For each object \(A\) in \(C_R\) we set \[\operatorname{Cov}(A)=\text{ the set of all surjective arrows }\varphi:A'\to A \text{ in }C_R\text{ with target }A.\] It clearly defines a pretopology and in turn we obtain a site \(C_R\). For each object \(A\) in \(C_R\), we shall denote by \(C_{A|R}\) the category of arrows in \(C_R\) with target \(A\). We may and shall provide the category \(C_{A|R}\) with the topology induced from the forgetful functor \(j_A:C_{A|R}\to C_R\). We note that the category \(C_{A|R}\) has a final object (\(1_A:A\to A\)) and an initial object (\(i_A:R\to A\)), and admits a fibre-product as well as coproduct. Henceforth we shall denote the object (\(B\to A\)) in \(C_{A|R}\), by abuse of notation, simply by \(B\) in case when there is no possible confusion. Given an abelian category \(\mathcal C\), a \(\mathcal C\)-valued sheaf on \(C_{A|R}\) is, by definition, a functor \(F:C_{A|R}^o\to\mathcal C\) such that, for any surjective arrow \(B'\to B\) in \(C_{A|R}\), \[0\to F(B)\to F(B')\rightrightarrows F(B'\mathbin{\mathop{\times}\limits_B}B')\] is exact. The additive presheaves are those which preserve coproduct i.e., \(F(B_1\otimes_R B_2)=F(B_1)\oplus F(B_2)\). For example, an \(A\)-module \(M\) defines the functor \[\operatorname{Der}_R(-,M):C_{A|R}^o\to(A\text{-mod})\] given by \(B\mapsto\operatorname{Der}_R(B,M)\) = \(R\)-linear derivations of \(B\) with values in \(M\) regarded as \(B\)-module via the structural map \(B\to A\), and it is clearly an additive sheaf on \(C_{A|R}^o\), where (\(A\text{-mod}\)) denotes the category of \(A\)-modules. A presheaf \(F\) on \(C_{A|R}\) with values in (\(A\text{-mod}\)) is called “finite type” if (F\(B\)) is an \(A\)-module of finite type whenever \(B\) is an \(R\)-algebra of finite type. For example, if \(A\) is noetherian and \(M\) is an \(A\)-module of finite type, then \(\operatorname{Der}_R(-,M)\) is an additive sheaf of finite type on the site \(C_{A|R}\).
A few more words on notations and basic facts in homological algebra:

3.1\(a\) Throughout the rest of this section, we shall denote by \(\widetilde C_{A|R}\) (or \(C_{A|R}^{\vee}\)) the category of sheaves (or presheaves) with values in (\(A\text{-mod}\)). We have the (left exact) injection functor \(i_{A|R}:\widetilde C_{A|R}\to C_{A|R}^{\vee}\), and the associated-sheaf \(exact\) functor \(a_{A|R}:C_{A|R}^{\vee}\to\widetilde C_{A|R}\) which is adjoint to the functor \(i_{A|R}\). An object \(B\in\operatorname{ob}(C_{A|R})\) induces a continuous functor \(j:C_{B|R}\to C_{A|R}\) and in turn a commutative diagram
\[\begin{CD} \widetilde C_{A|R} @>{\widetilde j}>> \widetilde C_{B|R}\\ @V{i_{A|R}}VV @VVV\\ C_{A|R}^{\vee} @>{j^{\vee}}>> C_{B|R}^{\vee}. \end{CD}\tag{*}\]

where the restriction functor \(\widetilde j\) (as well as \(j^{\vee}\)) is an exact functor, since the site \(C_{B|R}\) is nothing but the category of arrows in \(C_{A|R}\) having target \(B\), with the induced topology. A ring homomorphism \(S\to R\) induces a continuous functor \(\beta:C_{A|R}\to C_{A|S}\), and in turn a commutative diagram
\[\begin{CD} \widetilde C_{A|S} @>{\widetilde\beta}>> \widetilde C_{A|R}\\ @V{i_{A|S}}VV @VVV\\ C_{A|S}^{\vee} @>{\beta^{\vee}}>> C_{A|R}^{\vee}. \end{CD}\tag{**}\]
Note that \(\widetilde\beta\) is not necessarily an exact functor.

3.1\(b\) The right derived functor of the injection functor \(i_{A|R}:\widetilde C_{A|R}\to C_{A|R}^{\vee}\) will be denoted by \(\underline H_{A|R}^{\bullet}\). For each sheaf \(F\) on \(C_{A|R}\), we set \[H^o(A|R,F)=[\underline H_{A|R}^o(F)](A).\] For any \(B'\to B\) in \(\operatorname{Cov}(B)\), let us define the functor \[H^o((B'\to B),-):C_{A|R}^{\vee}\to(A\text{-mod})\] by \[H^o((B'\to B),F)=\operatorname{Ker}\bigl(F(B')\rightrightarrows F(B'\mathbin{\mathop{\times}\limits_B}B')\bigr).\] The right derived functors of \(H^o((B'\to B),-)\) will be denoted by \(H^{\bullet}((B'\to B),-)\). One knows that the \(H^{\bullet}((B'\to B),F)\) are the cohomology groups of the \(Čech\) semi-simplicial module \[F(B')\rightrightarrows F(B'\mathbin{\mathop{\times}\limits_B}B') \triplerightarrow F(B'\mathbin{\mathop{\times}\limits_B}B' \mathbin{\mathop{\times}\limits_B}B')\cdots.\] The right derived functor of \(\underline H_{A|R}^{\vee o}:C_{A|R}^{\vee}\to C_{A|R}\) will be denoted by \(\underline H_{A|R}^{\vee\bullet}\), where \[[\underline H_{A|R}^{\vee o}(F)](B)= \varinjlim_{\{B'\to B\}\in\operatorname{Cov}(B)}H^o((B'\to B),F).\] For each presheaf \(F\) on \(C_{A|R}\), we set \[\check H^{\bullet}(A|R,F)=[\underline H_{A|R}^{\vee\bullet}(F)](A) =\varinjlim_{\{A'\to A\}\in\operatorname{Cov}(A)}H^{\bullet}((A'\to A),F).\] For any sheaf \(F\) on \(C_{A|R}\), we have the usual spectral sequence \[\check H^p(A|R,\underline H_{A|R}^q(F))\Rightarrow H^{p+q}(A|R,F)\] where we note that \(\check H^o(A|R,\underline H^q(F))=0\) for all \(q>0\). In particular \[\begin{cases} \check H^1(A|R,F)=H^1(A|R,F),\\ 0\to\check H^2(A|R,F)\to H^2(A|R,F)\to \check H^1(A|R,\underline H_{A|R}^1(F))\to\check H^3(A|R,F) \quad\text{is exact.} \end{cases}\]

3.1\(c\) If \(B\) is an object in \(C_{A|R}\), the commutative diagram \(*\) in 2.1\(a\) together with the fact that \(\widetilde j\) as well as \(j^{\vee}\) are exact functors yields the canonical isomorphism \[H^{\bullet}(B|R,\widetilde jF)=[\underline H_{A|R}^{\bullet}(F)](B).\] On the other hand, a ring homomorphism \(S\to R\) induces the commutative diagram \(**\) in 2.1\(a\). Now \(\widetilde\beta\) is not necessarily an exact functor. However, \(\beta\) preserves a fibre-product, and thus we obtain a spectral sequence \[\underline H_{A|R}^p(R^q\widetilde\beta F)\Rightarrow \beta\underline H_{A|S}^{p+q}(F),\quad\text{i.e.,}\quad H^p(A|R,R^q\widetilde\beta F)\Rightarrow H^{p+q}(A|S,F).\] Since we have the canonical isomorphism \(\widetilde\beta=a_{A|R}\widetilde\beta^o i_{A|S}\) and \(\beta^{\#}=a_{A|R}\beta^{\vee}\) is an exact functor, we have \(R^q\widetilde\beta=\beta^{\#}\underline H_{A|S}^q(F)\). Thus the spectral sequence above can now be written as \[H^p(A|R,\beta^{\#}\underline H_{A|S}^q(F))\Rightarrow H^{p+q}(A|S,F).\]

blocks.title: Lemma 3.2.
blocks.html:
Lemma 3.2.

Given \(B\in\operatorname{ob}(C_{A|R})\), choose a surjective arrow \(P\to B\) in \(C_{B|R}\), where \(P\) is a polynomial algebra over \(R\). Then for any \(F\in\operatorname{ob}(C_{A|R}^{\vee})\), the natural map \(H^{\bullet}((P\to B),F)\to\check H^{\bullet}(F)(B)\) is an isomorphism.

Proof. It suffice to see that the natural map \[H^o((P\to B),F)\to\check H^{\vee o}(F)(B)= \varinjlim_{\{B'\to B\}\in\operatorname{Cov}(B)}H^o((B'\to B),F)\] is an isomorphism, which is clear since, for any \(\{B'\to B\}\in\operatorname{Cov}(B)\), there exists an arrow \(\{P\to B\}\to\{B'\to B\}\).

blocks.title: Corollary 3.3.
blocks.html:
Corollary 3.3.
  1. \(a\) Assume that \(A\) is noetherian. If \(F\in\operatorname{ob}(\widetilde C_{A|R})\) is of finite type, then \(\underline H^n(F)\) is of finite type for all \(n\).
  2. \(b\) For any polynomial algebra \(P\) over \(R\), we have \(H^n(P|R,F)=0\) for all \(n>0\) and for all \(F\in\operatorname{ob}(\widetilde C_{P|R})\).
  3. \(c\) A diagram \(0\to F'\to F\to F''\to0\) in \(\widetilde C_{A|R}\) is exact if and only if \(0\to F'(P)\to F(P)\to F''(P)\to0\) is exact for every \(P\in\operatorname{ob}(C_{A|R})\) which is a polynomial algebra over \(R\).

Proof. \(a\) In view of the spectral sequence \[\check H^p(B|R,\underline H^q(F))\Rightarrow H^{p+q}(B|R,F)\] together with the fact that \(\check H^{\vee o}(\underline H^q(F))=0\) for all \(q>0\), it suffices to show that \(\underline H^n(G)\) is of finite type for all \(n\) and for all presheaf \(G\) of finite type. However \(\check H^{\vee}(B|R,G)=H^{\bullet}((P\to B),G)\), where \(P\) is a polynomial algebra over \(R\) and \({P\to B}\in\operatorname{Cov}(B)\), and furthermore \(H^{\bullet}((P\to B),G)\) is simply the cohomology gotten from the complex \[\cdots G(P\mathbin{\mathop{\times}\limits_B}P \mathbin{\mathop{\times}\limits_B}P) \triplearrows G(P\mathbin{\mathop{\times}\limits_B}P) \rightrightarrows G(P).\] If \(B\) is an \(R\)-algebra of finite type, then we may take \(P\) to be of finite type over \(R\) so that \(P\mathbin{\mathop{\times}\limits_B}\cdots \mathbin{\mathop{\times}\limits_B}P\) are all \(R\)-algebras of finite type. If \(G\) is of finite type, then (G\(P\mathbin{\mathop{\times}\limits_B}\cdots \mathbin{\mathop{\times}\limits_B}P\)) are all \(A\)-modules of finite type, so that \(H^{\bullet}((P\to B),G)\) is an \(A\)-module of finite type since \(A\) is noetherian.

\(b\) Let \(P\) be a polynomial algebra over \(R\). If \(B\to P\) is any surjective \(R\)-algebra map, then it admits a section and hence \(H^i((B\to P),G)=0\) for all \(i>0\), so that \(\check H^{\vee i}(P|R,G)=0\) for all \(i>0\) and for all presheaves \(G\) on \(C_{P|R}\). It follows that the spectral sequence \(\check H^{\vee p}(P|R,\underline H_{P|R}^q(F))\Rightarrow H^{p+q}(P|R,F)\) degenerates and hence \(\check H^{\vee o}(P|R,\underline H^n(F))\simeq H^n(P|R,F)\) for all \(n\). However \(\check H^{\vee o}(P|R,\underline H^n(F))=0\) for all \(n>0\) whenever \(F\) is a sheaf, and hence \(H^n(P|R,F)=0\) for all \(n>0\) and for all \(F\in\operatorname{ob}(\widetilde C_{P|R})\).

\(c\) If \(0\to F'\to F\to F''\to0\) is an exact sequence in \(\widetilde C_{A|R}\), then \[0\to F'(B)\to F(B)\to F''(B)\to H^1(B|R,F')\to H^1(B|R,F)\to\cdots\] is exact. If \(P\in\operatorname{ob}(C_{A|R})\) is a polynomial algebra over \(R\), then \(H^1(P|R,F')=0\) by 3.3\(b\) and hence \[0\to F'(P)\to F(P)\to F''(P)\to0\] is exact. As for the other direction, let \(P_{A|R}\) be the full subcategory of \(C_{A|R}\) in which \[\operatorname{ob}(P_{A|R})=\{P\in\operatorname{ob}(C_{A|R})\mid P\text{ is a polynomial algebra over }R\},\] provided with the induced topology, and let \(p:P_{A|R}\to C_{A|R}\) be the inclusion functor. Since every object in \(C_{A|R}\) can be covered by an object in \(P_{A|R}\), it follows from a comparison lemma \(　　　　\) that \(\widetilde p:\widetilde C_{A|R}\to\widetilde P_{A|R}\) is an equivalence of categories. Consequently, if \(0\to F'(P)\to F(P)\to F''(P)\to0\) is exact for every \(P\in\operatorname{ob}(P_{A|R})\), then \(0\to\widetilde pF'\to\widetilde pF\to\widetilde pF''\to0\) is exact in \(\widetilde P_{A|R}\) and hence \(0\to F'\to F\to F''\to0\) is exact in \(\widetilde C_{A|R}\).

Remark 3.4. Every surjective arrow in \(P_{A|R}\) admits a section. It follows immediately that every presheaf on \(P_{A|R}\) is actually a sheaf i.e., \(\widetilde P_{A|R}=P^\vee_{A|R}\). Consequently we find that the restriction functor \(\widetilde C_{A|R}\to P^\vee_{A|R}\) is an equivalence of categories.

blocks.title: Lemma 3.5.
blocks.html:
Lemma 3.5. Consider a diagram of \(R\)-algebras \[\xymatrix{B'\ar[r]^-{\mathrm{surjective}}&B&C\ar[l]}\] and let \(S\) be an \(R\)-algebra.

(i) If \(\operatorname{Tor}_1^R(B,S)=0\), then the canonical map \[(B'\mathbin{\mathop\times\limits_B}C)\otimes_RS\to(B'\otimes_RS)\mathbin{\mathop\times\limits_{B\otimes_RS}}(C\otimes_RS)\] is an isomorphism.

(ii) Assume that \(B'\) is \(R\)-flat. If \(\operatorname{Tor}_i^R(C,S)=0\) for all \(0<i<n\) and \(\operatorname{Tor}_i^R(B,S)=0\) for all \(0<i<n\), then \(\operatorname{Tor}_i^R(B'\mathbin{\mathop\times\limits_B}C,S)=0\) for all \(0<i<n\).

Proof: Let \(0\to J\to B'\to B\to0\) be exact.

(i) If \(\operatorname{Tor}_1^R(B,S)=0\), then \[0\to J\otimes_RS\to B'\otimes_RS\to B\otimes_RS\to0\] is exact so that \[0\to J\otimes_RS\to(B'\otimes_RS)\mathbin{\mathop\times\limits_{B\otimes_RS}}(C\otimes_RS)\to C\otimes_RS\to0\] is exact. We thus obtain an exact commutative diagram \[\xymatrix{J\otimes_RS\ar[r]\ar@{=}[d]&(B'\mathbin{\mathop\times\limits_B}C)\otimes_RS\ar[r]\ar[d]&C\otimes_RS\ar[r]\ar@{=}[d]&0\\0\ar[r]&J\otimes_RS\ar[r]&(B'\otimes_RS)\mathbin{\mathop\times\limits_{B\otimes_RS}}(C\otimes_RS)\ar[r]&C\otimes_RS\ar[r]&0}\] and therefore the canonical map is an isomorphism.

(ii) Since \(B'\) is \(R\)-flat and \(\operatorname{Tor}_i^R(B,S)=0\) for all \(0<i\leq n\) by hypothesis, we have \(\operatorname{Tor}_i^R(J,S)=0\) for all \(0<i<n\). Then the exact sequence \[0\to J\to B'\mathbin{\mathop\times\limits_B}C\to C\to0\] together with the hypothesis on \(C\) entails the assertion.

blocks.title: Corollary 3.6.
blocks.html:
Corollary 3.6.

\(a\) Let \(A,S\) be \(R\)-algebras. If \(\operatorname{Tor}_1^R(A,S)=0\), then the canonical map \[\check H^\bullet(A\otimes_RS\mid S,G)\to\check H^\bullet(A\mid R,SG)\] is an isomorphism for any \(G\in\operatorname{ob}(C^\vee_{A\otimes_RS|S})\), where \(S:C_{A|R}\to C_{A\otimes_RS|S}\) is given by \(X\mapsto X\otimes_RS\). In particular if \(S\) is \(R\)-flat, then \(S\check H^\bullet(G)\to\check H^\bullet(SG)\) is an isomorphism for every \(G\).

\(b\) Let \(P\to B\) be a surjective \(R\)-algebra map, where \(P\) is a polynomial algebra over \(R\). If \(\operatorname{Tor}_i^R(B,S)=0\) for all \(0<i\leq n\), then \[\operatorname{Tor}_i^R(P\mathbin{\mathop\times\limits_B}\cdots\mathbin{\mathop\times\limits_B}P,S)=0\quad\text{for all }0<i<n.\]

Proof: \(a\) Given \(B\in\operatorname{ob}(C_{A|R})\), choose \(\{P\to B\}\in\operatorname{Cov}(B)\), where \(P\) is polynomial over \(R\). By 3.5(i), \[(P\mathbin{\mathop\times\limits_B}\cdots\mathbin{\mathop\times\limits_B}P)\otimes_RS\to(P\otimes_RS)\mathbin{\mathop\times\limits_{B\otimes_RS}}\cdots\mathbin{\mathop\times\limits_{B\otimes_RS}}(P\otimes_RS)\] is an isomorphism, and hence \[\check H^\bullet(B\mid R,SG)=\check H^\bullet(\{P\to B\},SG)=\check H^\bullet(\{P\otimes_RS\to B\otimes_RS\},G)=\check H^\bullet(B\otimes_RS\mid S,G).\] If \(S\) is \(R\)-flat this applies to every \(X\).

\(b\) Set \(P^{(m)}=\underbrace{P\mathbin{\mathop\times\limits_B}\cdots\mathbin{\mathop\times\limits_B}P}_{m+1}\). If \(m=0\) there is nothing to prove since \(P\) is \(R\)-flat. Since \(P^{(m)}=P\mathbin{\mathop\times\limits_B}P^{(m-1)}\), the assertion follows inductively from 3.5(ii).

blocks.title: Proposition 3.7.
blocks.html:
Proposition 3.7.

\(a\) Let \(A,S\) be \(R\)-algebras. If \(\operatorname{Tor}_i^R(A,S)=0\) for all \(0<i\leq n\), then for any sheaf \(F\) on \(C_{A\otimes_RS|S}\), the canonical map \[H^i(A\otimes_RS\mid S,F)\to H^i(A\mid R,\widetilde{SF})\] is an isomorphism for all \(i\leq n\), where \(S(X)=X\otimes_RS\).

\(b\) Let \(P\) be a polynomial algebra over \(R\). Then for any additive sheaf \(F\) on \(C_{A\otimes_RP|R}\), the canonical map \[H^n(A\otimes_RP\mid R,F)\to H^n(A\mid R,F)\] is an isomorphism for all \(n>0\).

\(c\) Let \(S\to R\to A\) be ring homomorphisms. Then for any additive sheaf \(F\) on \(C_{A|S}\), we have an exact sequence \[0\to H^0(A\mid R,F_R)\to H^0(A\mid S,F)\to H^0(R\mid S,F)\to H^1(A\mid R,F_R)\to H^1(A\mid S,F)\to H^1(R\mid S,F)\to\cdots\] \[\cdots\to H^n(A\mid R,F_R)\to H^n(A\mid S,F)\to H^n(R\mid S,F)\to H^{n+1}(A\mid R,F_R)\to\cdots\] where \(F_R(X)=\operatorname{Ker}(F(X)\to F(R))\) for all \(X\in\operatorname{ob}(C_{A|R})\), via the forgetful functor \(C_{A|R}\to C_{A|S}\).

Proof: \(a\) Consider \(S:C_{A|R}\to C_{A\otimes_RS|S}\). Since \(S\) sends polynomial algebras over \(R\) to polynomial algebras over \(S\), \(\widetilde S\) is exact by 3.3\(c\), and we obtain the commutative diagram of spectral sequences \[\xymatrix{E_S^{p,q}(S(X))=\check H^p(X\otimes_RS\mid S,\underline H^q(F))\ar@{=>}[r]\ar[d]&H^{p+q}(X\otimes_RS\mid S,F)\ar[d]\\E_R^{p,q}(X)=\check H^p(X\mid R,\underline H^q(\widetilde{SF}))\ar@{=>}[r]&H^{p+q}(X\mid R,\widetilde{SF}).}\] The map on \(E\)-terms is an isomorphism whenever \(\operatorname{Tor}_1^R(X,S)=0\), by 3.6\(a\). Induction on \(n\), using 3.6\(b\), shows that it is an isomorphism for all \(q<n\); since the two \(E^{0,n}\) terms are zero, the asserted map in degree \(n\) is an isomorphism.

\(b\) Let \(f:C_{A|R}\to C_{A\otimes_RP|R}\) be given by \(f(X)=X\otimes_RP\). If \(P'\to X\) is a polynomial cover, then \(P'\otimes_RP\to X\otimes_RP\) is again one. Since \(P\) is flat, 3.5(i) gives \[\check H^\bullet(X\mid R,\check fG)=\check H^\bullet(\{P'\to X\},\check fG)=\check H^\bullet(\{P'\otimes_RP\to X\otimes_RP\},G)=\check H^\bullet(X\otimes_RP\mid R,G).\] Thus \(\widetilde f\) sends acyclic objects to acyclic objects and is exact by 3.2\(c\). For each \(X\), \[(\widetilde fF)(X)=F(X\otimes_RP)\to F(X)\oplus F(P).\] For additive \(F\) this is an isomorphism. Therefore \[H^n(A\mid R,\widetilde fF)\simeq H^n(A\mid R,F\oplus F(P))=H^n(A\mid R,F)\] for \(n>0\), since the constant sheaf \(F(P)\) is flask.

\(c\) (communicated from Rinehart) For the forgetful functor \(\beta:C_{A|R}\to C_{A|S}\), we have a spectral sequence \[E^{p,q}=H^p(A\mid R,\beta^\#\underline H^q_{A|S}(F))\Longrightarrow H^{p+q}(A\mid S,F),\] where \(\beta^\#=a_{A|R}\beta^*\). If \(F\) is additive, \(\beta^\#\underline H^q_{A|S}(F)\) is constant for \(q>0\): for polynomial \(P\) over \(R\), write \(P=R\otimes_SP'\), and 3.7\(b\) gives \(H^q(P\mid S,F)\simeq H^q(R\mid S,F)\). Hence \(E^{p,q}=0\) for \(p,q>0\), yielding \[(*)\quad0\to E^{1,0}\to H^1\to E^{0,1}\to E^{2,0}\to H^2\to E^{0,2}\to E^{3,0}\to H^3\to\cdots.\] Here \(E^{0,q}\simeq H^q(R\mid S,F)\) for \(q>0\), while \(E^{p,0}=H^p(A\mid R,\widetilde{\beta F})\). The exact sequence \(0\to F_R\to\widetilde{\beta F}\to F(R)\to0\), combined with (*), gives the desired exact sequence.

blocks.title: Corollary 3.8.
blocks.html:
Corollary 3.8.

\(a\) If \(A,B\) are \(R\)-algebras such that \(\operatorname{Tor}_i^R(A,B)=0\) for all \(0<i\leq n\), then, for any additive sheaf \(F\in\operatorname{ob}(\widetilde C_{A\otimes_RB\mid R})\), the canonical map \[H^i(A\otimes_RB\mid R,F)\to H^i(A\mid R,F)\oplus H^i(B\mid R,F)\] is an isomorphism for all \(i\leq n\).

\(b\) Let \(A\) be an \(R\)-algebra such that \(\operatorname{Tor}_i^R(A,A)=0\) for all \(i>0\). If \(A\otimes_RA\to A\) is an isomorphism, then for any additive sheaf \(F\in\operatorname{ob}(\widetilde C_{A\mid R})\), we have \(H^i(A\mid R,F)=0\) for all (i).

Proof: \(a\) Let \(F\in\operatorname{ob}(\widetilde C_{A\otimes_RB\mid R})\) be additive. By 3.7\(c\), the ring homomorphisms \(R\to A\to A\otimes_RB\) gives us the exact sequence \[(*)\qquad\cdots\to H^i(A\otimes_RB\mid A,F_A)\to H^i(A\otimes_RB\mid R,F)\to H^i(A\mid R,F)\to\cdots.\] Consider now the commutative diagram \[\xymatrix{H^i(A\otimes_RB\mid A,F_A)\ar[r]\ar[d]&H^i(A\otimes_RB\mid R,F)\ar[d]\\H^i(B\mid R,\widetilde{A F_A})\ar[r]^-{\sim}&H^i(B\mid R,F)}\] where the functor \(A:C_{B\mid R}\to C_{A\otimes_RB\mid A}\) is given by \(X\mapsto A\otimes_RX\), and the map \(H^i(B\mid R,\widetilde{A F_A})\to H^i(B\mid R,F)\) is induced from the map \(\widetilde{A F_A}\to F\), which is an isomorphism since \(F\) is additive. Note that the commutativity of the above diagram, because of its functorial nature, is reduced to the case \(i=0\).

If \(\operatorname{Tor}_i^R(A,B)=0\) for all \(0<i\leq n\), then \(H^i(A\otimes_RB\mid A,F_A)\to H^i(B\mid R,\widetilde{A F_A})\) is an isomorphism for all \(i\leq n\) by 3.7\(a\), and hence the composite map \[H^i(A\otimes_RB\mid A,F_A)\to H^i(A\otimes_RB\mid R,F)\to H^i(B\mid R,F)\] and (because of the symmetry) \[H^i(A\otimes_RB\mid B,F_B)\to H^i(A\otimes_RB\mid R,F)\to H^i(A\mid R,F)\] are isomorphisms for all \(i\leq n\). This implies that the exact sequence (*) breaks up to short split-exact sequences \[0\to H^i(A\otimes_RB\mid A,F_A)\to H^i(A\otimes_RB\mid R,F)\to H^i(A\mid R,F)\to0\] for all \(i\leq n\), and that \[H^i(A\otimes_RB\mid R,F)\to H^i(A\mid R,F)\oplus H^i(B\mid R,F)\] is an isomorphism for all \(i\leq n\).

\(b\) Since \(\operatorname{Tor}_i^R(A,A)=0\) for all \(i>0\), it follows from 3.8\(a\) that \[H^i(A\otimes_RA\mid R,F)\to H^i(A\mid R,F)\oplus H^i(A\mid R,F)\] is an isomorphism for all (i). On the other hand, \(A\otimes_RA\to A\) is an isomorphism by hypothesis and hence \(H^i(A\mid R,F)\to H^i(A\otimes_RA\mid R,F)\) is an isomorphism for all (i). This means that the diagonal map \(H^i(A\mid R,F)\to H^i(A\mid R,F)\oplus H^i(A\mid R,F)\) is an isomorphism for all (i), i.e., \(H^i(A\mid R,F)=0\) for all (i).

blocks.title: Corollary 3.9.
blocks.html:
Corollary 3.9. Let \(S\) be a multiplicative subset of an \(R\)-algebra \(A\). If \(F\in\operatorname{ob}(\widetilde C_{S^{-1}A\mid R})\) is additive such that \(F_A\) is also additive, then the canonical map \[H^i(S^{-1}A\mid R,F)\to H^i(A\mid R,F)\] is an isomorphism for all (i), where \(F_A\in\operatorname{ob}(\widetilde C_{S^{-1}A\mid A})\) is defined by \(F_A(X)=\operatorname{Ker}(F(X)\to F(A))\) for all \(X\in\operatorname{ob}(C_{S^{-1}A\mid A})\).

Proof: Since \(F\) is additive, the ring homomorphisms \(R\to A\to S^{-1}A\) gives us the exact sequence \[\cdots\to H^n(S^{-1}A\mid A,F_A)\to H^n(S^{-1}A\mid R,F)\to H^n(A\mid R,F)\to\cdots.\] On the other hand, since \(S^{-1}A\otimes_AS^{-1}A\to S^{-1}A\) is an isomorphism and \(F_A\) is additive by hypothesis, it follows from 3.8\(b\) that \(H^n(S^{-1}A\mid A,F_A)=0\) for all \(n\), and hence \(H^n(S^{-1}A\mid R,F)\to H^n(A\mid R,F)\) is an isomorphism for all \(n\).

Now let \(A\) be an \(R\)-algebra. An \(A\)-module \(M\) defines an additive sheaf \(\operatorname{Der}_R(-,M)\in\operatorname{ob}(\widetilde C_{A\mid R})\) by \(X\mapsto\operatorname{Der}_R(X,M)\). We now make the following definition:
Definition 3.10: Let \(A\) be an \(R\)-algebra. For each \(A\)-module \(M\) we set \[D^i(A\mid R,M)=H^i(A\mid R,\operatorname{Der}_R(-,M)).\]

We want to make an explicit computation of low-dimensional ones i.e., \(D^i(A\mid R,M)\) for \(i=1,2\). Choose a surjective arrow \(P\to A\) in \(C_{A\mid R}\) where \(P\) is a polynomial algebra over \(R\), and let \(0\to I\to P\to A\to0\) be exact. Then

blocks.title: Lemma 3.10:
blocks.html:
Lemma 3.10: \[D^1(A\mid R,M)=\operatorname{Coker}(\operatorname{Der}_R(P,M)\to\operatorname{Hom}_P(I,M))\] \[D^2(A\mid R,M)=H^1(A\mid R,\underline H^1_{A\mid R}(\operatorname{Der}_R(-,M))).\]

Proof: Consider the simplicial algebra \[(*)\qquad\cdots P_A^{(2)}\triplearrows P_A^{(1)}\rightrightarrows P_A^{(0)}\] where \(P_A^{(n)}=\underbrace{P\times_A\cdots\times_AP}_{n+1}\). For each \(n\) we have the exact sequence \[0\to I^{(n)}\to P_A^{(n)}\to A\to0\] where \(I^{(n)}=\underbrace{I\times_I\cdots\times_I I}_{n+1}\), and it is easy to see that

\(a\): \[0\to\operatorname{Hom}_A(\bar I^{(n)}/\Delta(\bar I),M)\to\operatorname{Der}_R(P_A^{(n)},M)\xrightarrow{\operatorname{Der}(\Delta,M)}\operatorname{Der}_R(P,M)\to0\] is exact for all \(n\), where \(\bar I=I/I^2\) and \(\Delta\) stands for the diagonal map. We also note the exact sequence

\(b\): \[0\to\operatorname{Hom}_A(\bar I^{(n)}/\Delta(\bar I),M)\to\operatorname{Hom}_A(\bar I^{(n)},M)\xrightarrow{\operatorname{Hom}(\Delta,M)}\operatorname{Hom}_A(\bar I,M)\to0.\] The exact sequences \(a\) and \(b\) yield the respective exact sequences of simplicial \(A\)-modules. Since \(\{I\to0\}\), \(\{I\to I\}\) admits sections, we have \[H^i(\{I\to0\},\operatorname{Hom}_A(-,M))=H^i(\{I\to I\},\operatorname{Hom}_A(-,M))=0\] for all \(i>0\), and it follows readily that \[\begin{cases}0\to\operatorname{Der}_R(A,M)\to\operatorname{Der}_R(P,M)\to\operatorname{Hom}_A(\bar I,M)\to H^1(\{P\to A\},\operatorname{Der}_R(-,M))\to0&\text{ is exact},\\H^i(\{P\to A\},\operatorname{Der}_R(-,M))=0&\text{ for all }i>1.\end{cases}\] It follows from 3.1\(b\) that \[D^1(A\mid R,M)=H^1(A\mid R,\operatorname{Der}_R(-,M))=H^1(\{P\to A\},\operatorname{Der}_R(-,M))\] \[=\operatorname{Coker}(\operatorname{Der}_R(P,M)\to\operatorname{Hom}_A(\bar I,M))=\operatorname{Coker}(\operatorname{Der}_R(P,M)\to\operatorname{Hom}_P(I,M)),\] \[D^2(A\mid R,M)=H^1(A\mid R,\underline H^1_{A\mid R}(\operatorname{Der}_R(-,M))).\]

Now choose a free \(P\)-module \(F\) and a \(P\)-linear map \(F\xrightarrow\gamma P\) such that \(\gamma(F)=I\). Associated to \(P\)-linear map \(\gamma:F\to P\), there is a Koszul complex, which is simply denoted, by abuse of notation, by \(K_I\).

blocks.title: Lemma 3.11:
blocks.html:
Lemma 3.11: \(D^2(A\mid R,M)\simeq\operatorname{Coker}(H^1(K_I,M)\to\operatorname{Hom}_A(H_1(K_I),M))\).

Proof: We have \[D^2(A\mid R,M)=H^1(A\mid R,\underline H^1_{A\mid R}(\operatorname{Der}_R(-,M)))\] \[=\operatorname{Ker}(H^1(P_A^{(1)}\mid R,M)\rightrightarrows H^1(P_A^{(2)}\mid R,M))\] since \([\underline H^1_{A\mid R}(\operatorname{Der}_R(-,M))](P_A^{(0)})=[\underline H^1_{A\mid R}(\operatorname{Der}_R(-,M))](P)=0\).

Let \(0\to K\to F\xrightarrow\gamma I\to0\) be exact. If we set (P[F]) to be the symmetric algebra over \(P\) generated by \(F\), then (P[F]) and \(P[F]\otimes_RP[F]\) are polynomial algebras over \(R\) and we obtain the surjective arrows \[P[F]\to P_A^{(1)}=P\times_AP\quad\text{given by}\quad X\mapsto(0,\gamma(X)),\] \[P[F]\otimes_RP[F]\to P_A^{(2)}=P\times_AP\times_AP,\quad X\otimes1\mapsto(0,\gamma(X),0),\quad1\otimes X\mapsto(0,0,\gamma(X)),\] where \(X\in F\). If we set \(J=\operatorname{Ker}(P[F]\to P_A^{(1)})\) and \(J'=\operatorname{Ker}(P[F]\otimes_RP[F]\to P_A^{(2)})\), then \[J=(F)\cap(F_\gamma)=(K)+(F\cdot F_\gamma),\] \[J'=(F\otimes1+1\otimes F)\cap(F\otimes1+1\otimes F_\gamma)\cap(F_\gamma\otimes1+1\otimes F_\gamma)\] \[=(K\otimes1+1\otimes K)+(F\cdot F_\gamma\otimes1+1\otimes F\cdot F_\gamma)+(F\otimes F).\]

Now \(\pi_i:P_A^{(2)}\to P_A^{(1)}\), \(i=0,1,2\), can be lifted to \(\widehat\pi_i:P[F]\otimes_RP[F]\to P[F]\) where, for each \(X\in F\), \[\pi_0:\begin{cases}X\otimes1\mapsto\gamma(X)-X\\1\otimes X\mapsto X,\end{cases}\qquad \pi_1:\begin{cases}X\otimes1\mapsto0\\1\otimes X\mapsto X,\end{cases}\qquad \pi_2:\begin{cases}X\otimes1\mapsto X\\1\otimes X\mapsto0.\end{cases}\] A trivial computation shows that \(\pi_0-\pi_1+\pi_2\) sends \(K\otimes1+1\otimes K+1\otimes F\cdot F_\gamma\) to zero, whereas it sends \(F\cdot F_\gamma\otimes1+F\otimes F\) onto \(F\cdot F_\gamma\). Now for any \(D\in\operatorname{Der}_R(P[F]\otimes_RP[F],M)\), \(D(F\cdot F_\gamma\otimes1+F\otimes F)=0\) since \(I\cdot M=0\), and consequently \[D^2(A\mid R,M)=\operatorname{Ker}(D^1(P_A^{(1)}\mid R,M)\rightrightarrows D^1(P_A^{(2)}\mid R,M))\] \[=\{[f]\in D^1(P_A^{(1)}\mid R,M)\mid f(F\cdot F_\gamma)=0\}\] \[=\operatorname{Coker}(\operatorname{Der}_R(P[F],M)\to\operatorname{Hom}_{P[F]}((F)\cap(F_\gamma)/(F)\cdot(F_\gamma),M))\] \[=\operatorname{Coker}(\operatorname{Der}_P(P[F],M)\to\operatorname{Hom}_{P[F]}((F)\cap(F_\gamma)/(F)\cdot(F_\gamma),M)).\] Now \[0\to(F)\to P[F]\xrightarrow{P[0]}P\to0,\qquad0\to(F_\gamma)\to P[F]\xrightarrow{P[\gamma]}P\to0\] are exact and hence \[(F)\cap(F_\gamma)/(F)\cdot(F_\gamma)=\operatorname{Tor}_1^{P[F]}(P,P_\gamma)=H_1(K_I),\] where \(K_I\) is the Koszul complex associated to the \(P\)-linear map \(F\xrightarrow\gamma I(\subset P)\). We thus have \[D^2(A\mid R,M)=\operatorname{Coker}(\operatorname{Hom}_P(F,M)\to\operatorname{Hom}_A(H_1(K_I),M))\] \[=\operatorname{Coker}(H^1(K_I,M)\to\operatorname{Hom}_A(H_1(K_I),M)).\]
We can now state all the basic facts needed for our purpose, summarizing the various results we have already established.

blocks.title: Theorem 3.12.
blocks.html:
Theorem 3.12.

(1) If \(0\to M'\to M\to M''\to0\) is an exact sequence of \(A\)-modules, then \[0\to D^0(A\mid R,M')\to D^0(A\mid R,M)\to D^0(A\mid R,M'')\to D^1(A\mid R,M')\to\] \[D^1(A\mid R,M)\to D^1(A\mid R,M'')\to\cdots\to D^n(A\mid R,M')\to D^n(A\mid R,M)\to D^n(A\mid R,M'')\to\cdots\] is exact.

(2) Let \(S\to R\to A\) be ring homomorphisms. Then for any \(A\)-module \(M\) we have the exact sequence \[0\to D^0(A\mid R,M)\to D^0(A\mid S,M)\to D^0(R\mid S,M)\to D^1(A\mid R,M)\to\] \[D^1(A\mid S,M)\to D^1(R\mid S,M)\to\cdots\to D^n(A\mid R,M)\to D^n(A\mid S,M)\to D^n(R\mid S,M)\to\cdots.\]

(3) Let \(A,S\) be \(R\)-algebras such that \(\operatorname{Tor}_i^R(A,S)=0\) for all \(i>0\). Then for any \(A\otimes_RS\)-module \(M\), the canonical map \[D^i(A\otimes_RS\mid S,M)\to D^i(A\mid R,M)\] is an isomorphism for all (i). In particular, if \(A\) is \(R\)-flat we have \[D^i(A\otimes_RS\mid S,M)\simeq D^i(A\mid R,M)\] for all (i) and for all \(R\)-algebras \(S\).

(4) Let \(S\) be a multiplicative subset of \(A\). Then for any \(S^{-1}A\)-module \(N\), the canonical map \[D^i(S^{-1}A\mid R,N)\to D^i(A\mid R,N)\] is an isomorphism for all (i). Consequently, for any \(A\)-module \(M\) we have the canonical isomorphism \[D^i(S^{-1}A\mid R,S^{-1}M)\simeq S^{-1}D^i(A\mid R,M)\] for all (i).

(5) If \(A,B\) are \(R\)-algebras such that \(\operatorname{Tor}_i^R(A,B)=0\) for all \(0<i\leq n\), then for any \(A\otimes_RB\)-module \(M\), the canonical map \[D^i(A\otimes_RB\mid R,M)\to D^i(A\mid R,M)\oplus D^i(B\mid R,M)\] is an isomorphism for all \(i\leq n\). In particular, if \(\operatorname{Tor}_i^R(A,A)=0\) for all \(i>0\) and \[\Delta:\operatorname{Spec}(A)\to\operatorname{Spec}(A)\mathbin{\mathop\times\limits_{\operatorname{Spec}(R)}}\operatorname{Spec}(A)\] is an open immersion \(for instance (A/R) étale\), then \(D^i(A\mid R,M)=0\) for all \(i>0\) and for all \(A\)-modules \(M\).

(6) Let \(A\) be an \(R\)-algebra of essentially finite type over \(R\), where \(R\) is noetherian, and let \(E\) be a flat \(A\)-module. Then we have a canonical isomorphism \[E\otimes_A D^i(A\mid R,M)\simeq D^i(A\mid R,E\otimes_AM)\] for all \(i\geq0\) and for all \(A\)-modules \(M\).

(7) If \(R\) is noetherian and \(A\) is an \(R\)-algebra of essentially finite type, then \(D^i(A\mid R,M)\) is \(A\)-module of finite type for all (i), provided \(M\) is an \(A\)-module of finite type.

(8) Let \(P\) be a polynomial algebra over \(R\) and let \(0\to I\to P\to A\to0\) be exact. Then \(D^0(A\mid R,M)=\operatorname{Der}_R(A,M)\), \[D^1(A\mid R,M)=\operatorname{Coker}(\operatorname{Der}_R(P,M)\to\operatorname{Hom}_A(I/I^2,M))\] is the set of isomorphism classes of \(R\)-algebra extensions of \(A\) by \(M\), \[D^2(A\mid R,M)=\operatorname{Coker}(H^1(K_I,M)\to\operatorname{Hom}_A(H_1(K_I),M)).\]

Proof: (1) If \(0\to M'\to M\to M''\to0\) is an exact sequence of \(A\)-modules, then for any polynomial algebra \(P\) over \(R\), \(0\to\operatorname{Der}_R(P,M')\to\operatorname{Der}_R(P,M)\to\operatorname{Der}_R(P,M'')\to0\) is exact and hence \(0\to\operatorname{Der}_R(-,M')\to\operatorname{Der}_R(-,M)\to\operatorname{Der}_R(-,M'')\to0\) is an exact sequence of sheaves by 3.3\(c\).

(2) \(operatorname{Der}_S(-,M)\) is an additive sheaf on \(C_{A\mid S}\), and \[\operatorname{Der}_R(B,M)=\operatorname{Ker}(\operatorname{Der}_S(B,M)\to\operatorname{Der}_S(R,M))\] for every \(B\in\operatorname{ob}(C_{A\mid R})\). Therefore our assertion follows from 3.7\(c\).

(3) Let \(S:C_{A\mid R}\to C_{A\otimes_RS\mid S}\) be the functor given by \(X\mapsto X\otimes_RS\). Then \[\widetilde S\operatorname{Der}_S(-,M)=\operatorname{Der}_R(-,M)\] and hence our assertion follows from 3.7\(a\).

(4) Follows from 3.9. Indeed, \(F=\operatorname{Der}_R(-,N)\) is an additive sheaf on \(C_{S^{-1}A\mid R}\), and \(F_A\in\operatorname{ob}(C_{S^{-1}A\mid A})\) is also additive since \[F_A(X)=\operatorname{Ker}(F(X)\to F(A))=\operatorname{Ker}(\operatorname{Der}_R(X,N)\to\operatorname{Der}_R(A,N))=\operatorname{Der}_A(X,N),\] i.e., \(F_A=\operatorname{Der}_A(-,N)\).

(5) The first assertion follows from 3.8\(a\). Now assume that \(\operatorname{Tor}_i^R(A,A)=0\) for all \(i>0\) and that \[\Delta:\operatorname{Spec}(A)\to\operatorname{Spec}(A)\mathbin{\mathop\times\limits_{\operatorname{Spec}(R)}}\operatorname{Spec}(A)\] is an open immersion i.e., \((A\otimes_RA)_{\varphi^{-1}(\mathfrak m)}\to A_{\mathfrak m}\) is an isomorphism for all maximal ideals \(\mathfrak m\) of \(A\), where \(\varphi:A\otimes_RA\to A\) is the multiplication map. Then the first assertion combined with 3.12(4) entails that the diagonal map \[\Delta_{\mathfrak m}:D^i(A\mid R,M)_{\mathfrak m}\to D^i(A\mid R,M)_{\mathfrak m}\oplus D^i(A\mid R,M)_{\mathfrak m}\] is an isomorphism for every maximal ideal \(\mathfrak m\) of \(A\) and hence \[\Delta:D^i(A\mid R,M)\to D^i(A\mid R,M)\oplus D^i(A\mid R,M)\] is an isomorphism, which entails that \(D^i(A\mid R,M)=0\) for all (i).

(6) In view of 3.12(4), we may assume that \(A\) is an \(R\)-algebra of finite type. Now consider the map \[E\otimes_A\operatorname{Der}_R(-,M)\to\operatorname{Der}_R(-,E\otimes_AM)\] in \(C_{A\mid R}^{\sim}\). Since \(E\) is \(A\)-flat, we have an isomorphism \[E\otimes_A\operatorname{Der}_R(B,M)\xrightarrow{\sim}\operatorname{Der}_R(B,E\otimes_AM)\] whenever \(B\in\operatorname{ob}(C_{A\mid R})\) is of finite type over \(R\). It follows from 3.3\(b\) that \[D^i(A\mid R,E\otimes_A\operatorname{Der}_R(-,M))\to D^i(A\mid R,E\otimes_AM)\] is an isomorphism. On the other hand, the functor \(E\otimes_A-:(A\text{-mod})\to(A\text{-mod})\) is exact, and therefore we have \[E\otimes_A D^i(A\mid R,M)=D^i(A\mid R,E\otimes_A\operatorname{Der}_R(-,M)),\] and hence our assertion.

(7) follows from 3.3\(a\).

(8) In view of 3.10 and 3.11, it suffices to show that \[\operatorname{Coker}(\operatorname{Der}_R(P,M)\to\operatorname{Hom}_A(I/I^2,M))=\operatorname{Exalcomm}(A\mid R,M),\] where \(operatorname{Exalcomm}(A\mid R,M)\) denotes the set of isomorphism classes of \(R\)-algebra extensions of \(A\) by \(M\). Let \(0\to M\to A'\to A\to0\) be an \(R\)-algebra extension of \(A\) by \(M\). Since \(P\) is a polynomial algebra over \(R\), we obtain a commutative diagram \[\xymatrix{0\ar[r]&I\ar[r]\ar[d]&P\ar[r]\ar[d]&A\ar[r]\ar@{=}[d]&0\\0\ar[r]&M\ar[r]&A'\ar[r]&A\ar[r]&0}\] where \(I\to M\) is uniquely determined by the extension \(A'\) up to \(operatorname{Der}_R(P,M)\). We thus obtain a map \[\Phi:\operatorname{Exalcomm}(A\mid R,M)\to\operatorname{Coker}(\operatorname{Der}_R(P,M)\to\operatorname{Hom}_A(I/I^2,M)).\] Conversely a \(P\)-linear map \(I\to M\) yields an extension by push-out \[\xymatrix{0\ar[r]&I\ar[r]\ar[d]&P\ar[r]\ar[d]&A\ar[r]\ar@{=}[d]&0\\0\ar[r]&M\ar[r]&A'\ar[r]&A\ar[r]&0.}\] Two \(P\)-linear maps \(I\rightrightarrows M\) which differ by \(operatorname{Der}_R(P,M)\) is trivially seen to yield isomorphic extensions and therefore we obtain a map \[\Psi:\operatorname{Coker}(\operatorname{Der}_R(P,M)\to\operatorname{Hom}_A(I/I^2,M))\to\operatorname{Exalcomm}(A\mid R,M).\] It is straightforward to verify that \(\Phi,\Psi\) are inverse process to each other.

blocks.title: Corollary 3.13.
blocks.html:
Corollary 3.13.

\(a\) \(R\)-algebra \(A\) is formally smooth over \(R\) if and only if \(D^1(A\mid R,M)=0\) for every \(A\)-module \(M\).

\(b\) Let \(R\) be noetherian and \(A\) an \(R\)-algebra of finite type. Then \(A\) is a relative complete-intersection over \(R\) if and only if \(D^2(A\mid R,M)=0\) for every \(A\)-module \(M\).

\(c\) Let \(R\) be noetherian and \(A\) an \(R\)-algebra of finite type. Then for any multiplicative subset \(S\) in \(A\), we have a canonical isomorphism \[D^i(S^{-1}A\mid R,S^{-1}M)\simeq S^{-1}D^i(A\mid R,M)\quad\text{for all }i\geq0.\]

\(d\) Let \(R\) be noetherian and \(A\) a local \(R\)-algebra of essentially finite type. We denote by \(\widehat A\) the \(\mathfrak m\)-adic completion of \(A\) where \(\mathfrak m\) = the maximal ideal of \(A\). Then for any \(A\)-module \(E\) of finite type we have a canonical isomorphism \[D^i(A\mid R,\widehat A\otimes_AE)\simeq\widehat A\otimes_A D^i(A\mid R,E).\]

Proof: \(a\) follows immediately from \(D^1(A\mid R,M)=\operatorname{Exalcomm}(A\mid R,M)\).

\(b\) Let \(P\) be a polynomial algebra over \(R\) in a finite number of variables and let \(0\to I\to P\to A\to0\) be exact. Localizing if necessary, we may assume that \(I\) is generated by a \(P\)-regular sequence which would imply that \(H_1(K_I)=0\) and therefore \[D^2(A\mid R,M)=\operatorname{Coker}(H^1(K_I,M)\to\operatorname{Hom}_A(H_1(K_I),M))=0.\] Conversely assume that \(D^2(A\mid R,M)=0\) for every \(A\)-module \(M\).

Let \(0\to I\to P\to A\to0\) be exact where \(P\) is a polynomial algebra over \(R\) in a finite number of variables. We have \[0=D^2(A\mid R,M)=\operatorname{Coker}(H^1(K_I,M)\to\operatorname{Hom}_A(H_1(K_I),M))\] i.e., \(H^1(K_I,M)\to\operatorname{Hom}_A(H_1(K_I),M)\) is surjective for all \(A\)-module \(M\). Choose an exact sequence \(0\to K\to F\to I\to0\) where \(F\) is a free \(P\)-module of finite type. We note that \(H_1(K_I)=K/K_0\) where \(K_0=\operatorname{Im}(\bigwedge^2F\to F)\). The fact that \(D^2(A\mid R,H_1(K_I))=0\) i.e., \[H^1(K_I,H_1(K_I))\to\operatorname{Hom}_A(H_1(K_I),H_1(K_I))\text{ is surjective}\] entails that \(0\to K/K_0\to F/IF\to I/I^2\to0\) is exact and splits as \(A\)-modules and in particular \(I/I^2\) is projective as \(A\)-module. Therefore, localizing \(A\) if necessary, we may assume that \(I/I^2\) is \(A\)-free, and that \(F/IF\to I/I^2\) is an isomorphism. Then the fact that \(0\to K/K_0\to F/IF\to I/I^2\to0\) is exact and splits as \(A\)-modules entails that \(H_1(K_I)=K/K_0=0\). This means that \(I\) is generated by a \(P\)-regular sequence i.e., \(A\) is a relative complete intersection over \(R\).

\(c\) follows immediately from 3.12(4) and (6).

\(d\) Firstly it follows from 3.12(6) that \[D^i(A\mid R,\widehat A\otimes_AE)\simeq\widehat A\otimes_A D^i(A\mid R,E)\] is an isomorphism for all (i), and therefore it suffices to show that

\[D^1(\widehat A\mid R,\widehat A\otimes_AE)\to D^1(A\mid R,\widehat A\otimes_AE)\] is an isomorphism. Our proof is based on the description of \(D^1\) in terms of algebra extensions. Let \[0\to\widehat A\otimes_AE\to A'\xrightarrow{\varphi}A\to0\] be an \(R\)-algebra extension. The fact that \(\widehat A\otimes_AE\) is an \(\widehat A\)-module of finite type entails readily that \(A'\) is a local \(R\)-algebra, complete with respect to \(\mathfrak m'\)-adic topology where \(\mathfrak m'\) = the maximal ideal of \(A'\). Therefore if there is an \(R\)-algebra map \(\psi:A\to A'\) such that \(\varphi\circ\psi=\operatorname{id}_A\), then \(\psi\) extends uniquely to \(\widehat\psi:\widehat A\to A'\) such that \(\varphi\circ\widehat\psi=\operatorname{id}_{\widehat A}\). This proves that \[D^1(\widehat A\mid R,\widehat A\otimes_AE)\to D^1(A\mid R,\widehat A\otimes_AE)\] is injective. Now let \[0\to\widehat A\otimes_AE\to B\xrightarrow{\varphi}A\to0\] be an \(R\)-algebra extension. Firstly we claim that the topology on \(\widehat E=\widehat A\otimes_AE\) induced from \(\mathfrak m_B\)-adic topology on \(B\) coincides with the topology on \(\widehat E\) as \(\widehat A\)-module. Indeed, since \(A\) is essentially of finite type over \(R\), one can find a subalgebra \(B_0\) of \(B\) such that \(B_0\) is a local \(R\)-algebra of essentially finite type over \(R\) and that \(\varphi(B_0)=A\). Set \(E_0=\widehat E\cap B_0\) and denote by \(\mathfrak m_B,\mathfrak m_0,\mathfrak m\) the maximal ideals of \(B,B_0,A\) respectively. Then \(\mathfrak m_B=\mathfrak m_0+\widehat E\) and hence \(\mathfrak m_B^{n+1}=\mathfrak m_0^{n+1}+\mathfrak m^n\widehat E\) for all \(n\). Since \(B_0\) is noetherian, there exists an integer \(r>0\) by Artin-Rees Theorem such that \[ \begin{aligned} \widehat E\cap\mathfrak m_B^{n+1} &=\mathfrak m^n\widehat E+\widehat E\cap\mathfrak m_0^{n+1}\\ &=\mathfrak m^n\widehat E+\mathfrak m^{n+1-r}(E_0\cap\mathfrak m_0^r) \subset\mathfrak m^{n+1-r}\widehat E \end{aligned} \] for all \(n\geq r\), which proves our assertion about the two topologies on \(E\). In particular, \(\widehat E=\widehat A\otimes_AE\) is complete with respect to the topology induced from \(\mathfrak m_B\)-adic topology on \(B\), and hence we obtain the exact commutative diagram \[ \xymatrix{ 0\ar[r]&\widehat E\ar[r]\ar@{=}[d]&B\ar[r]\ar[d]&A\ar[r]\ar[d]&0\\ 0\ar[r]&\widehat E\ar[r]&\widehat B\ar[r]&\widehat A\ar[r]&0 } \] where \(\widehat B\) stands for \(\mathfrak m_B\)-adic completion of \(B\). This proves that \(D^1(\widehat A\mid R,\widehat E)\to D^1(A\mid R,\widehat E)\) is surjective and hence we are done.

blocks.title: Corollary 3.14.
blocks.html:
Corollary 3.14.

Let \(R\) be noetherian and \(A\) an \(R\)-algebra of finite type, smooth everywhere except at one closed point \(x\in\operatorname{Spec}(A)\). We then have isomorphisms \[D^1(A\mid R,A)\simeq D^1(A_x\mid R,A_x)\simeq D^1(\widehat A_x\mid R,\widehat A_x).\]

Proof: Since \(A\) is smooth over \(R\) except at \(x\in\operatorname{Spec}(A)\), \(D^1(A\mid R,A)\) is an \(A\)-module of finite type with its support on the set \(\{x\}\), and therefore we have \[D^1(A\mid R,A)=D^1(A\mid R,A)_x=\widehat{D^1(A\mid R,A)}_x.\] Therefore our assertion follows from 3.13.

Remark 3.15. Let \(R\) be topological ring and \(A\) a topological \(R\)-algebra. We then define, for any \(A\)-module \(E\), \[D^i_{\mathrm{top}}(A\mid R,E)=\varprojlim_\alpha D^i(A_\alpha\mid R_\alpha,A_\alpha\otimes_AE)\] where \(A_\alpha=A/J_\alpha\), \(R_\alpha=R/I_\alpha\), and \(J_\alpha,I_\alpha\) are both open in \(A,R\) respectively. Then \(A\) is formally smooth topological \(R\)-algebra means that \(D^1_{\mathrm{top}}(A\mid R,E)=0\) for every \(A\)-module \(E\).

Now let \(X\) be a scheme over a ring \(R\) and \(E\) a quasi-coherent \(X\)-module. We then define the sheaves \[\underline D^i_{X\mid R}(E)=D^i(X\mid R,E)\] on \(X\) via presheaves on \(X\) given by \[[\underline D^i_{X\mid R}(E)](U)=D^i(\Gamma(U,\mathcal O_X)\mid R,\Gamma(U,E))\] for every affine open set \(U\subset X\). If \(R\) is noetherian and \(X\) is of finite type over \(R\), then the property 3.13\(a\) shows that \(\underline D^i_{X\mid R}(E)\) can actually be constructed canonically, as a quasi-coherent \(X\)-module.

In case when there is no possible confusion we shall abreviate by setting \(\underline D^i_{X\mid R}=\underline D^i_{X\mid R}(\mathcal O_X)\). All the statements of 3.13 can now be translated in terms of these quasi-coherent sheaves \(\underline D^i_{X\mid R}(E)\), which we leave to the readers. We list below a few properties which is most relevant to our purpose.

blocks.title: Corollary 3.15.
blocks.html:
Corollary 3.15.

Let \(R\) be noetherian and \(X\) an \(R\)-scheme of finite type. Then

\(a\) For any coherent \(X\)-module \(E\), \(\underline D^i_{X\mid R}(E)\) is again a coherent \(X\)-module for all (i), and furthermore \[\operatorname{Supp}\underline D^i_{X\mid R}(E)\subset X^{\mathrm{Sing}}\quad\text{for all }i>0\] where (X^{\mathrm{Sing}}=\{x\in X\mid X\text{ is non-smooth over }R\text{ at the point }x\in X\}).

\(b\) If \(X\) is flat over \(R\), then for any \(R\)-algebra \(S\), we have the canonical isomorphism \[\underline D^i_{X\otimes_RS\mid S}(E)\simeq\underline D^i_{X\mid R}(E)\] for all (i).

\(c\) If \(X\) is a relative complete intersection over \(R\), then for every surjective ring homomorphism \(S\to R\) we have the exact sequence \[0\to\underline D^1_{X\mid R}(E)\to\underline D^1_{X\mid S}(E) \to\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_{\mathcal O_X}(I/I^2\otimes_R\mathcal O_X,E)\to0\] where \(I=\operatorname{Ker}(S\to R)\). If, furthermore, \(R\) is local with the residue field \(k\) and \(X\) is \(R\)-flat, then we have the exact sequence \[0\to\underline D^1_{X_0\mid k}(\mathcal O_{X_0})\to \underline D^1_{X\mid S}(\mathcal O_{X_0})\to D^1(R\mid S,k)\otimes_k\mathcal O_{X_0}\to0\] where \(X_0=X\otimes_Rk\).

Proof: \(a\) Follows from 3.12(6) and (8) whereas \(b\) follows from 3.12(3).

\(c\) Since \(X\) is a relative complete-intersection over \(R\), we have \(\underline D^i_{X\mid R}(E)=0\) for all \(i\geq2\) by 3.12(10) and hence \[0\to\underline D^0_{X\mid R}(E)\to\underline D^0_{X\mid S}(E) \to\underline D^0_{R\mid S}(E)\to\underline D^1_{X\mid R}(E) \to\underline D^1_{X\mid S}(E)\to\underline D^1_{R\mid S}(E)\to0\] is exact by 3.12(2), where \(\underline D^i_{R\mid S}(E)\) are quasi-coherent sheaves of \(X\) defined by \[[\underline D^i_{R\mid S}(E)](U)=D^i(R\mid S,E(U))\] for every affine open subset \(U\subset X\). In particular, \(\underline D^0_{R\mid S}(E)=0\) since \(S\to R\) is surjective, and \[\underline D^1_{R\mid S}(E)= \underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_{\mathcal O_X}(I/I^2\otimes_R\mathcal O_X,E)\] by 3.12(7), and hence the first exact sequence. If \(X\) is \(R\)-flat, we have \[\underline D^i_{X_0\mid k}(E)\simeq\underline D^i_{X\mid R}(E)\] for all (i) and for all quasi-coherent \(X_0\)-module \(E\) and in particular \[\underline D^1_{X_0\mid k}(\mathcal O_{X_0})\simeq \underline D^1_{X\mid R}(\mathcal O_{X_0})\] from which the second exact sequence follows.

Remark 3.16: The quasi-coherent sheaves \(\underline D^i_{X\mid R}(E)\) we have defined should appear as \(E_2^{0,i}\) of a spectral sequence relating the local ones and the global ones. L. Illusie has obtained such a theory (Lecture Notes in Mathematics 239). His method is via “homotopical algebra” generalizing the methods of M. André and D. Quillen rather than “cohomological algebra”. It seems that the approach we have taken here can be globalized via Grothendieck cohomology on the category of schemes with an appropriate topology generalizing the one on the affine category.
4. Formal moduli of deformations

One amongst the nice cofibered categories in which the isomorphism classes of objects are usually not prorepresentable, but nevertheless admit a formal versal object is the cofibered category of deformations. We now come back to the situations and notations of paragraph 1. Thus, throughout the section, \(\Lambda\) is a fixed complete local noetherian ring with the residue field \(k\), and \(C_\Lambda\) is the category of artinian local \(\Lambda\)-algebras with the same residue field \(k\).

Let \(X_0\) be a fixed scheme over the field \(k\). By an (infinitesimal) deformation of \(X_0\), we mean a pair \((R,X)\) where \(R\in\operatorname{ob}(C_\Lambda)\) and \(X\) is a flat \(R\)-scheme together with a commutative diagram \[ \xymatrix{ X_0\ar[r]^{i_X}\ar[d]&X\ar[d]^{\mathrm{flat}}\\ {\operatorname{Spec}(k)}\ar[r]&{\operatorname{Spec}(R)} } \]

such that the induced morphism \(X_0\to X\times_{\operatorname{Spec}(R)}\operatorname{Spec}(k)\) is an isomorphism. We note that, since \(R\) is an artinian local ring, \(i_X:X_0\to X\) is necessarily a closed immersion which is a homeomorphism on underlying topological spaces. If \((R,X)\), \((R',X')\) are deformations of \(X_0\) to \(R,R'\) respectively, we define an arrow \((R',X')\to(R,X)\) to consist of a map \(R'\xrightarrow{\varphi}R\) in \(C_\Lambda\) and a morphism \(\Phi:X\to X'\) of schemes with a commutative diagram \[\xymatrix@C=4em@R=2em{X\ar[rr]^{\Phi}\ar[ddd]&&X'\ar[ddd]\\ &X_0\ar[ul]_{i_X}\ar[ur]^{i_{X'}}\ar[d]&\\ &{\operatorname{Spec}(k)}\ar[dl]\ar[dr]&\\ {\operatorname{Spec}(R)}\ar[rr]_{\operatorname{Spec}(\varphi)}&&{\operatorname{Spec}(R')}}\]

We thus obtain the category \(M_{X_0}\) of (infinitesimal) deformations of \(X_0\) in which the objects and the arrows are defined as above. The category \(M_{X_0}\) is provided with a functor \(p:M_{X_0}\to C_\Lambda\) defined by \((R,X)\mapsto R\). Now let \((R,X)\in\operatorname{ob}(M_{X_0})\) and a map \(R\xrightarrow{\varphi}S\) in \(C_\Lambda\) be given. We then obtain a commutative diagram \[\xymatrix@C=5em@R=3em{{X\times_{\operatorname{Spec}(R)}\operatorname{Spec}(S)}\ar[r]^{p_1}\ar[d]_{\mathrm{flat}}&X\ar[d]^{\mathrm{flat}}\\{\operatorname{Spec}(S)}\ar[r]_{\operatorname{Spec}(\varphi)}&{\operatorname{Spec}(R)}}\] and \(\varphi\)-arrow \((\varphi,p_1):(R,X)\to(S,X\times_{\operatorname{Spec}(R)}\operatorname{Spec}(S))\) is obviously cocartesian in \(M_{X_0}\). It follows that the category \(M_{X_0}\) is a cofibered category over \(C_\Lambda\) via \(P:M_{X_0}\to C_\Lambda\). We observe the following facts:

4.1\(a\): Let \((1_R,\Phi):(R,X_1)\to(R,X_2)\) be an arrow in \(M_{X_0}\) i.e., \(\Phi:X_2\to X_1\) is a morphism over \(R\) such that \(X_2\otimes_Rk\to X_1\otimes_Rk\) is an isomorphism. Since \(R\) is artinian and \(X_i\) is \(R\)-flat, it follows immediately that \(\Phi\) is an isomorphism so that \((1_R,\Phi):(R,X_1)\to(R,X_2)\) is an isomorphism in \(M_{X_0}\). Consequently, \(M_{X_0}\) is a cofibered category in groupoids over \(C_\Lambda\).

4.1\(b\): Suppose that \(X_0\) is affine, say \(X_0=\operatorname{Spec}(A_0)\). Then for any object \((R,X)\in\operatorname{ob}(M_{X_0})\), \(X\) is also affine. Consequently, for any \(R\in\operatorname{ob}(C_\Lambda)\), the category \(M_{X_0}(R)\) is canonically equivalent, via the sections, to the category in which an object is a flat algebra \(A\) over \(R\) together with an \(R\)-algebra map \(j_A:A\to A_0\) inducing an isomorphism \(A\otimes_Rk\xrightarrow{\sim}A_0\), and a map \(\Phi:A_1\to A_2\) is an \(R\)-algebra map with a commutative diagram \[\xymatrix@C=5em@R=3em{A_1\ar[r]^{\Phi}\ar[d]_{j_{A_1}}&A_2\ar[d]^{j_{A_2}}\\A_0\ar@{=}[r]&A_0}\]

4.1\(c\): Consider the cofibered category \(\widehat M_{X_0}\) in groupoids over \(\widehat C_\Lambda\). By definition, the category \(\widehat M_{X_0}(R)\) for any \(R\in\operatorname{ob}(\widehat C_\Lambda)\) is canonically equivalent to the category of formal schemes \(\mathfrak X\), adic over the formal spectrum \(\operatorname{Spf}(R)\), such that \(X_n=\mathfrak X\otimes_RR/\mathfrak m^{n+1}\) is flat over \(R/\mathfrak m^{n+1}\) for every \(n\geq0\) (or what amounts to the same, if \(X_0\) is noetherian, such that \(\mathfrak X\) is flat over \(\operatorname{Spf}(R)\)). Consequently, if \(X_0\) is noetherian, \(\widehat M_{X_0}\) is canonically \(\widehat C_\Lambda\)-equivalent to the category of deformations of \(X_0\) over the objects in \(\widehat C_\Lambda\), in the sense of formal schemes.

4.1\(d\): Let \((R,X)\in\operatorname{ob}(M_{X_0})\) i.e., we have a commutative diagram \[\xymatrix@C=4em@R=3em{X_0\ar[r]^{i_X}\ar[d]&X\ar[d]^{\mathrm{flat}}\\{\operatorname{Spec}(k)}\ar[r]&{\operatorname{Spec}(R)}}\] such that \(X_0\to X\times_{\operatorname{Spec}(R)}\operatorname{Spec}(k)\) is an isomorphism. Now let \(U_0\subset X_0\) be a fixed open subscheme of \(X_0\). Since \(i_X:X_0\to X\) is a closed immersion which is a homeomorphism on the underlying topological spaces, we obtain a commutative diagram \[\xymatrix@C=4em@R=3em{U_0\ar[r]^{i_X|U_0}\ar[d]&X|U_0\ar[d]^{\mathrm{flat}}\\{\operatorname{Spec}(k)}\ar[r]&{\operatorname{Spec}(R)}}\] such that \(U_0\to(X|U_0)\times_{\operatorname{Spec}(R)}\operatorname{Spec}(k)=X\otimes_Rk|U_0\) is an isomorphism, so that \((R,X|U_0)\) is a deformation of \(U_0\) to \(R\). Furthermore, for any arrow \((\varphi,\Phi):(R,X)\to(R',X')\) in \(M_{X_0}\), \((\varphi,\Phi|U_0):(R,X|U_0)\to(R',X'|U_0)\) is an arrow in \(M_{U_0}\). Consequently we have the restriction functor \(M_{X_0}\to M_{U_0}\) over \(C_\Lambda\). One may also consider a localization as well as a formalization at a point. Namely let \(x_0\) be a fixed point in \(X_0\). If \((R,X)\in\operatorname{ob}(M_{X_0})\), then \(\operatorname{Spec}(\mathcal O_{X,x_0})\) is a deformation of \(\operatorname{Spec}(\mathcal O_{X_0,x_0})\) to \(R\) and \(\operatorname{Spec}(\widehat{\mathcal O}_{X,x_0})\) is a deformation of \(\operatorname{Spec}(\widehat{\mathcal O}_{X_0,x_0})\) over \(R\), where \(\widehat{\ }\) stands for the completion with respect to the linear topology given by the powers of the maximal ideal. We thus obtain functors \[M_{X_0}\to M_{\operatorname{Spec}(\mathcal O_{X_0,x_0})}\to M_{\operatorname{Spec}(\widehat{\mathcal O}_{X_0,x_0})}.\]
Firstly we establish that \(M_{X_0}\) is a homogeneous cofibered category over \(C_\Lambda\).

blocks.title: Lemma 4.2.
blocks.html:
Lemma 4.2. Consider a diagram
\[\xymatrix@C=5em@R=2em{R'\ar[d]&\\R\ar[r]_-{\mathrm{surjective}}&\bar R}\]

in \(C_\Lambda\), and let \(E,E'\) be flat modules over \(R,R'\) respectively. Then for any \(R'\)-homomorphism \(E'\to\bar E=E\otimes_R\bar R\), the fibre-product \(E\times_{\bar E}E'\) is flat as \(R\times_{\bar R}R'\)-module.

Proof: Let \(0\to I\to R\to\bar R\to0\) be exact so that \(0\to I\to R\times_{\bar R}R'\to R'\to0\) is exact. Since \(E\) is \(R\)-flat, \(0\to I\otimes_RE\to E\to\bar E\to0\) is exact and hence we obtain the exact sequence \[(*)\qquad0\to I\otimes_RE\to E\times_{\bar E}E'\to E'\to0.\] Set \(S=R\times_{\bar R}R'\). Since \(I\) is nilpotent, \(E\times_{\bar E}E'\) is flat as an \(S\)-module if and only if \((E\times_{\bar E}E')\otimes_SR'\) is flat as an \(R'\)-module and \(\operatorname{Tor}_1^S(E\times_{\bar E}E',R')=0\). However, we note that \(I\otimes_RE=I\otimes_S(E\times_{\bar E}E')\) and \(E'=R'\otimes_S(E\times_{\bar E}E')\) and therefore the exact sequence \((*)\) can be rewritten as \[0\to I\otimes_S(E\times_{\bar E}E')\to E\times_{\bar E}E'\to R'\otimes_S(E\times_{\bar E}E')\to0.\] This means that \(\operatorname{Tor}_1^S(R',E\times_{\bar E}E')=0\) and \(R'\otimes_S(E\times_{\bar E}E')=E'\) is flat as \(R'\)-module, and therefore \(E\times_{\bar E}E'\) is \(S\)-flat.

blocks.title: Corollary 4.3.
blocks.html:
Corollary 4.3. Given a diagram
\[\xymatrix@C=7em@R=3em{(R,X)\ar[dr]_{(\varphi,\Phi)}&(R',X')\ar[dl]^{(\varphi',\Phi')}\\&(\bar R,\bar X)&}\]

in \(M_{X_0}\), the amalgamated sum \(X\coprod_{\bar X}X'\) (in the sense of schemes) is a flat scheme over \(R\times_{\bar R}R'\), provided \(\varphi:R\to\bar R\) is surjective. Consequently, \(M_{X_0}\) is a homogeneous cofibered category in groupoids over \(C_\Lambda\).

Proof: For each affine open \(U_0\subset X_0\), we have by definition that \[X\coprod_{\bar X}X'|U_0\simeq\operatorname{Spec}\bigl(\Gamma(U_0,X)\times_{\Gamma(U_0,\bar X)}\Gamma(U_0,X')\bigr).\] Consequently, if \(\varphi:R\to\bar R\) is surjective, \(X\coprod_{\bar X}X'\) is a flat scheme over \(R\times_{\bar R}R'\) by 4.2, and the morphism \(i_X\coprod_{i_{\bar X}}i_{X'}:X_0\to X\coprod_{\bar X}X'\) induces an isomorphism \(X_0\to(X\coprod_{\bar X}X')\otimes_Sk\), where \(S=R\times_{\bar R}R'\). Therefore \((R\times_{\bar R}R',X\coprod_{\bar X}X')\in\operatorname{ob}(M_{X_0})\) and we obviously have that \[(R\times_{\bar R}R',X\coprod_{\bar X}X')=(R,X)\times_{(\bar R,\bar X)}(R',X')\] in the category \(M_{X_0}\), which means that \(M_{X_0}\) is homogeneous over \(C_\Lambda\).

blocks.title: Lemma 4.4.
blocks.html:
Lemma 4.4. For any scheme \(X_0\) over \(k\), we have a canonical exact sequence
\[0\to H^1(X_0,D^0_{X_0})\to[M_{X_0}(k[\epsilon])]\to H^0(X_0,D^1_{X_0})\]

where \(D^i_{X_0}\) are as defined in paragraph 3.

Proof: (Special case) Assume that \(X_0\) is affine and let \(\Gamma(X_0,\mathcal O_{X_0})=A_0\). Then as it was noted in 4.1\(b\), the category \(M_{X_0}(k[\epsilon])\) is canonically equivalent to the category \(E\), where

\(\operatorname{ob}(E)\): object in \(E\) is a flat algebra \(A\) over \(k[\epsilon]\) together with a \(k[\epsilon]\)-algebra map \(j_A:A\to A_0\) inducing the isomorphism \(k\otimes_{k[\epsilon]}j_A:k\otimes_{k[\epsilon]}A\xrightarrow{\sim}A_0\). Alternatively, an object in \(E\) is a \(k\)-algebra \(A\) together with an exact sequence \(0\to A_0\xrightarrow{\epsilon}A\xrightarrow{j_A}A_0\to0\) in which \(\epsilon(A_0)^2=0\) as an ideal in \(A\).

\(\operatorname{Fl}(E)\): An arrow \(A_1\to A_2\) in \(E\) with respect to first description of the object in \(E\) is a \(k[\epsilon]\)-algebra map \(\Phi:A_1\to A_2\) such that \(j_{A_1}=j_{A_2}\circ\Phi\). Therefore, an arrow \(A_1\to A_2\) in \(E\) with respect to the alternative description of objects in \(E\) is a \(k\)-algebra map \(\Phi:A_1\to A_2\) yielding a commutative diagram \[\xymatrix@C=4em@R=3em{0\ar[r]&A_0\ar[r]^{\epsilon}\ar@{=}[d]&A_1\ar[r]^{j_{A_1}}\ar[d]^{\Phi}&A_0\ar[r]\ar@{=}[d]&0\\0\ar[r]&A_0\ar[r]&A_2\ar[r]^{j_{A_2}}&A_0\ar[r]&0.}\] It follows that \([M_{X_0}(k[\epsilon])]\) is canonically isomorphic, via \(\Gamma(X_0,-)\), to the set of isomorphism classes of \(k\)-algebra extensions of \(A_0\) by \(A_0\). In other words, we have a canonical bijection \([M_{X_0}(k[\epsilon])]\xrightarrow{\sim}H^0(X_0,D^1_{X_0})\) via \(\Gamma(X_0,-)\), which is trivially seen to respect the vector space structures on both sides.

(General case) For each affine open \(U_0\subset X_0\), we get a canonical map \[[M_{X_0}(k[\epsilon])]\to H^0(U_0,D^1_{X_0})\] defined as the composite map \[[M_{X_0}(k[\epsilon])]\to[M_{U_0}(k[\epsilon])]\xrightarrow{\sim}H^0(U_0,D^1_{X_0})\] and hence we obtain a canonical map \[[M_{X_0}(k[\epsilon])]\to H^0(X_0,D^1_{X_0}).\] Its kernel is the set of isomorphism classes of locally-trivial deformations of \(X_0\) to \(k[\epsilon]\), i.e., the set of isomorphism classes of deformations of \(X_0\) to \(k[\epsilon]\), locally (on \(X\)) isomorphic to \(X_0\otimes_k k[\epsilon]\). Since \(D^0_{X_0}=\operatorname{Der}_k(\mathcal O_{X_0},\mathcal O_{X_0})= \operatorname{Aut}(X_0\otimes_k k[\epsilon]|X_0)\) = the sheaf of germs of automorphisms of \(X_0\otimes_k k[\epsilon]\) inducing the identity on the subscheme \(X_0\), it is canonically isomorphic to \(H^1(X_0,D^0_{X_0})\), so that we obtain the desired exact sequence.

blocks.title: Theorem 4.5.
blocks.html:
Theorem 4.5.

Let \(X_0\) be a scheme of finite type over \(k\) for which either (i) \(X_0\) is proper over \(k\) or (ii) \(X_0\) is affine but \(X_0^{\mathrm{Sing}}=\{x\in X_0\mid X_0\text{ is non-smooth over }k\text{ at the point }x\}\) is a finite set. Then

\(a\) \(M_{X_0}\) admits a formal versal object (called a formal versal deformation of \(X_0\)) over \(C_\Lambda\). If \((R,\mathfrak X)\) is a formal versal deformation of \(X_0\), then for any \((S,\mathfrak Y)\in\operatorname{ob}(\widehat M_{X_0})\), there is a map \((\varphi,\Phi):(R,\mathfrak X)\to(S,\mathfrak Y)\) in \(\widehat M_{X_0}\) such that the diagram below is cartesian

\[\xymatrix@C=5em@R=3em{\mathfrak Y\ar[r]^{\Phi}\ar[d]&\mathfrak X\ar[d]\\ {\operatorname{Spf}(S)}\ar[r]_{\operatorname{Spf}(\varphi)}&{\operatorname{Spf}(R)}}.\]

Furthermore a formal versal deformation \((R,\mathfrak X)\) of \(X_0\) prorepresents the functor \([M_{X_0}]\) if and only if, for every surjective map \(R'\to R\) in \(C_\Lambda\) and a deformation \((R',X')\) of \(X_0\) to \(R'\), the homomorphism

\[\operatorname{Aut}_{R'}(X'|X_0)\to \operatorname{Aut}_R(X'\otimes_{R'}R|X_0)\]

is surjective.

\(b\) If \(X_0\) is proper over \(k\), then \(M_{X_0}\) is smoothly pseudo-representable over \(C_\Lambda\).

Proof: \(a\) Since \(M_{X_0}\) is a homogeneous cofibered category in groupoids over \(C_\Lambda\) by 4.3, it suffices to see that \([M(k[\epsilon])]\) is a finite-dimensional vector space over \(k\) by 1.11 and 2.9\(b\).

Now consider the exact sequence \[(*)\qquad0\to H^1(X_0,D^0_{X_0})\to[M_{X_0}(k[\epsilon])] \to H^0(X_0,D^1_{X_0}).\] If \(X_0\) is proper over \(k\), then \(H^1(X_0,D^0_{X_0})\) and \(H^0(X_0,D^1_{X_0})\) are both finite dimensional since \(D^i_{X_0}\) are coherent for all \(i\geq0\) by 2.13\(a\). Now assume that \(X_0\) is affine such that \(X_0^{\mathrm{Sing}}\) is a finite set. Since \(D^1_{X_0}\) is a coherent sheaf on \(X_0\) such that \(\operatorname{Supp}D^1_{X_0}\subset X_0^{\mathrm{Sing}}\) by 2.13\(a\), we find that \(H^0(X_0,D^1_{X_0})\) is a finite-dimensional vector space over \(k\), whereas \(H^1(X_0,D^0_{X_0})=0\) since \(X_0\) is affine. Therefore, in either case, it follows from the exact sequence \((*)\) that \([M_{X_0}(k[\epsilon])]\) is a finite dimensional vector space over \(k\). The second statement of \(a\) follows from 2.7.

\(b\) If \(X_0\) is proper, \(\operatorname{Aut}(X_0\otimes_k k[\epsilon]|X_0) =H^0(X_0,D^0_{X_0})\) is a finite dimensional vector space and hence our statement follows from 2.13.

Remark 4.6\(a\): Let \(X_0\) be affine. Then a formal versal deformation of \(X_0\) exists if and only if \(D^1_{X_0}\) has its support on a finite number of points. However this does not imply that non-smooth points of \(X_0\) are all isolated. There are affine schemes \(X_0\) over \(k\) having a non-isolated non-smooth point but \(\operatorname{supp}D^1_{X_0}\) is a finite set.

Remark 4.6\(b\): Henceforth a deformation of \(X_0\) is meant by an object in \(M_{X_0}\). In case when there is a possible confusion, an object in \(M_{X_0}\) will be referred as an infinitesimal deformation of \(X_0\).

Remark 4.6\(c\): Let \(X_0\) be a scheme over \(k\). We have fixed a complete local noetherian ring \(\Lambda\) with the residue field \(k\), and considered the deformations of \(X_0\) over the category \(C_\Lambda\). (In practice one usually takes \(\Lambda=k\) or \(W(k)\) if \(k\) is a perfect field of positive characteristic.) Now \(C_k\subset C_\Lambda\) is a fully faithful imbedding, and that \(M_{X_0}|C_k\) is simply the category of deformations of \(X_0\) over \(S\) where \(S\in\operatorname{ob}(C_k)\). Therefore if \((R,\mathfrak X)\) is a formal versal deformation of \(X_0\) over \(C_\Lambda\), then \((k\otimes_\Lambda R,k\otimes_\Lambda\mathfrak X)\) is a formal versal deformation of \(X_0\) over \(C_k\). It follows, for example, that \(\dim_{C_\Lambda}M_{X_0}=\dim k\otimes_\Lambda R\) does not depend on the choice of \(\Lambda\) but depends only on \(X_0\).

One can generalize the above theorem in many ways. Let us for instance consider a pair \(Y_0\to X_0\) where \(X_0\) is a scheme of finite type over \(k\) and \(Y_0\) a fixed closed subscheme of \(X_0\). For each \(R\in\operatorname{ob}(C_\Lambda)\), a deformation of \((X_0,Y_0)\) to \(R\) is meant to be a commutative diagram
\[\xymatrix@C=4em@R=3em{Y_0\ar[r]\ar[d]\ar@/_1.5pc/[dd]&Y\ar[d]\ar@/^1.5pc/[dd]^{\mathrm{flat}}\\ X_0\ar[r]\ar[d]&X\ar[d]^{\mathrm{flat}}\\ {\operatorname{Spec}(k)}\ar[r]&{\operatorname{Spec}(R)}}\]

such that \((X_0,Y_0)\to(X\times_{\operatorname{Spec}(R)}\operatorname{Spec}(k), Y\times_{\operatorname{Spec}(R)}\operatorname{Spec}(k))\) is an isomorphism. Defining a morphism \((R',X',Y')\to(R,X,Y)\) of deformations of \((X_0,Y_0)\) to be the obvious ones, we obtain the cofibered category \(M_{X_0,Y_0}\) in groupoids over \(C_\Lambda\). (This is a process often used in rigidification of automorphisms in moduli problems of deformations.) One sees immediately, by the same argument as in 4.3 based on 4.2, that \(M_{X_0,Y_0}\) is a homogeneous cofibered category in groupoids over \(C_\Lambda\).

blocks.title: Lemma 4.7.
blocks.html:
Lemma 4.7.
\[0\to H^0(Y_0,\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_{\mathcal O_{Y_0}} (I/I^2,\mathcal O_{Y_0}))\to[M_{X_0,Y_0}(k[\epsilon])]\to[M_{X_0}(k[\epsilon])]\]

is exact, where \(I\) is the ideal sheaf of \(\mathcal O_{X_0}\) defining \(Y_0\) i.e.

\[0\to I\to\mathcal O_{X_0}\to\mathcal O_{Y_0}\to0\quad\text{is exact}.\]

Proof: Let \(B\) be the full subcategory of \(M_{X_0,Y_0}(k[\epsilon])\) whose objects are of the form \((k[\epsilon];X\times_{\operatorname{Spec}(k)} \operatorname{Spec}(k[\epsilon]),Y)\). We then have \[[B]=\operatorname{Ker}([M_{X_0,Y_0}(k[\epsilon])]\to[M_{X_0}(k[\epsilon])]).\] The category \(B\) is the groupoid of deformations of \(Y_0\) to \(k[\epsilon]\) “inside \(X_0\times_{\operatorname{Spec}(k)}\operatorname{Spec}(k[\epsilon])\)”, i.e., an object in \(B\) is a commutative diagram

\[\xymatrix@C=5em@R=3em{Y\ar[r]^{i_Y}\ar[dr]_{\mathrm{flat}}& {X_0\times_{\operatorname{Spec}(k)}\operatorname{Spec}(k[\epsilon])}\ar[dl]\\ &{\operatorname{Spec}(k[\epsilon])}}\]

such that \(\operatorname{Spec}(k)\times_{\operatorname{Spec}(k[\epsilon])}Y=i_{Y_0}\), and an arrow \((Y_1,i_{Y_1})\to(Y_2,i_{Y_2})\) is a \(k[\epsilon]\)-morphism \(\Phi:Y_1\to Y_2\) (necessarily an isomorphism) such that \(\operatorname{Spec}(k)\times_{\operatorname{Spec}(k[\epsilon])}\Phi=\operatorname{id}_{Y_0}\) and \(i_{Y_2}\circ\Phi=i_{Y_1}\). Alternatively, an object in \(B\) is a pair \((Y,j_Y)\), where \(Y\) is a deformation of \(Y_0\) to \(k[\epsilon]\) and \(j_Y:Y\to X_0\) is a morphism over \(k\) such that the composite morphism \(Y_0\to Y\xrightarrow{j_Y}X_0\) is \(i_{Y_0}\), and an arrow \((Y_1,j_{Y_1})\to(Y_2,j_{Y_2})\) is a morphism \(\Phi:Y_1\to Y_2\) such that \(k\otimes_{k[\epsilon]}\Phi=\operatorname{id}_{Y_0}\) and \(j_{Y_2}\circ\Phi=j_{Y_1}\). Therefore the association

\[\xymatrix@C=3em@R=2em{&0\ar[r]&I\ar[r]\ar[d]&\mathcal O_{X_0}\ar[r]\ar[d]^{j_Y}&\mathcal O_{Y_0}\ar[r]\ar@{=}[d]&0\\ (Y,j_Y)\ar@{|->}[r]&0\ar[r]&\mathcal O_{Y_0}\ar[r]^{\epsilon}&\mathcal O_Y\ar[r]&\mathcal O_{Y_0}\ar[r]&0}\]

gives rise to an isomorphism

\[[B]\xrightarrow{j|I}\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal O_{X_0}}(I,\mathcal O_{Y_0}) =\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal O_{Y_0}}(I/I^2,\mathcal O_{Y_0}) =H^0(Y_0,\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_{\mathcal O_{Y_0}} (I/I^2,\mathcal O_{Y_0})).\]

Remark 4.8. For a more general formula implying the above lemma, see SGA3 III 4.4. One may also note the following fact. One can define for any \(S\)-scheme \(X\) and a quasi-coherent \(X\)-module \(E\) a quasi-coherent \(X\)-module \(D^i_{X|S}(E)\) for all \(i\geq0\), generalizing the case when \(S\) is affine. As usual we simply write \(D^i_{X|S}(\mathcal O_X)=D^i_{X|S}\). We then have \[H^0(Y_0,\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_{\mathcal O_{Y_0}} (I/I^2,\mathcal O_{Y_0}))=H^0(Y_0,D^0_{Y_0|X})\] where \(0\to I\to\mathcal O_{X_0}\to\mathcal O_{Y_0}\to0\) is exact.

blocks.title: Theorem 4.9.
blocks.html:
Theorem 4.9.

Let \(X_0\) be \(k\)-scheme of finite type and \(Y_0\) a closed subscheme of \(X_0\). Assume that either (i) \(X_0\) is proper over \(k\) or (ii) \(X_0\) is affine and smooth over \(k\) outside a finite number of points and \(Y_0\) is proper over \(k\). We then have

\(a\) \(M_{X_0,Y_0}\) admits a formal versal deformation over \(C_\Lambda\), and it prorepresents the functor \([M_{X_0,Y_0}]\) if and only if, for every surjective map \(R'\to R\) in \(C_\Lambda\) and a deformation \((R';X',Y')\),

\[\operatorname{Aut}_{R'}((X',Y')|X_0)\to \operatorname{Aut}_R(X'\otimes_{R'}R,Y'\otimes_{R'}R|X_0)\]

is surjective.

\(b\) If furthermore \(H^0(X_0,D^0_{Y_0|k}|_{D^0_{X_0|k}(\mathcal O_{Y_0})} \times_{D^0_{X_0|k}}k)\) is finite dimensional, then \(M_{X_0,Y_0}\) is smoothly pseudo-representable.

Proof: It is obvious from 4.4 and 4.6 together with 1.11, 2.7, and 2.13.

We now consider the passage to localization and completion in the deformation theory of schemes. Firstly on a notation: Let \(X_0\) be a \(k\)-scheme of finite type. Given a fixed point \(x_0\in X_0\), we have natural functors
\[M_{X_0}\to M_{\operatorname{Spec}(\mathcal O_{X_0,x_0})} \to M_{\operatorname{Spec}(\widehat{\mathcal O}_{X_0,x_0})}.\]
and in turn we obtain the functors
\[\widehat M_{X_0}\to\widehat M_{\operatorname{Spec}(\mathcal O_{X_0,x_0})} \to\widehat M_{\operatorname{Spec}(\widehat{\mathcal O}_{X_0,x_0})}.\]

As it was remarked in 4.1\(c\), \(M_{X_0}\) is canonically equivalent to the category in which an object is a pair \((R,\mathcal X)\) where \(R\in\operatorname{ob}(\widehat C_\Lambda)\) and \(\mathcal X\) is a flat \(R\)-adic formal scheme together with a fixed isomorphism \(\mathcal X_0\xrightarrow\sim X_0\). Henceforth we shall write, when there is no possible confusion, the images of \((R,\mathcal X)\) under
\[M_{X_0}\to M_{\operatorname{Spec}(\mathcal O_{X_0,x_0})}\quad\text{and}\quad M_{X_0}\to M_{\operatorname{Spec}(\widehat{\mathcal O}_{X_0,x_0})}\]

by \((R,\mathcal X_{(x_0)})\) and \((R,\mathcal X_{(\widehat{x_0})})\) respectively. Therefore if \(\mathcal X=\varprojlim_ u X_ u\) where \(X_ u=\mathcal X imes_{\operatorname{Spf}(R)} \operatorname{Spec}(R/\mathfrak m^{ u+1})\), then
\[\mathcal X_{(x_0)}=\varprojlim_\nu\operatorname{Spec}(\mathcal O_{X_\nu,x_0}),\qquad \mathcal X_{(\widehat{x_0})}=\varprojlim_\nu \operatorname{Spec}(\widehat{\mathcal O}_{X_\nu,x_0}).\]

blocks.title: Theorem 4.10.
blocks.html:
Theorem 4.10.

Let \(X_0\) be an affine scheme of finite type over \(k\).

\(a\) If \(X_0\) is locally a complete-intersection, then \(M_{X_0}\) is smooth over \(C_\Lambda\).

\(b\) Assume that, for a fixed closed point \(x\in X_0\), \(X_0-\{x\}\) is smooth over \(k\). Then

\[M_{X_0}\to M_{\operatorname{Spec}(\mathcal O_{X_0,x})} \to M_{\operatorname{Spec}(\widehat{\mathcal O}_{X_0,x})}\]

are both minimally smooth functors. In particular, if \((R,\mathcal X)\) is a formal versal deformation of \(X_0\), then \((R,\mathcal X_{(x)})\) and \((R,\mathcal X_{(\widehat x)})\) are formal versal deformations of \(\operatorname{Spec}(\mathcal O_{X_0,x})\) and \(\operatorname{Spec}(\widehat{\mathcal O}_{X_0,x})\) respectively.

Proof: In this proof we go back to the notations of the cohomology of commutative algebras defined in the paragraph 3. We set \(X_0=\operatorname{Spec}(A_0)\).

\(a\) It suffices to show that \(P:[M_{X_0}]\to C_\Lambda\) is smooth.

Let \((R,\operatorname{Spec}(A))\) be a deformation of \(\operatorname{Spec}(A_0)\) to \(R\), where \(R\in\operatorname{ob}(C_\Lambda)\). Given a small extension \(R'\to R\) in \(C_\Lambda\) (i.e., \(0\to k\to R'\to R\to0\) is exact), we must show that \((R,\operatorname{Spec}(A))\) can be lifted to \(R'\). The exact sequence of \(D^i\) with coefficient \(A_0\) applied to the ring homomorphisms \(R'\to R\to A\) gives us the exact sequence

\[\cdots\to D^1(A|R,A_0)\to D^1(A|R',A_0)\to D^1(R|R',k)\otimes_k A_0\to D^2(A|R,A_0)\to\cdots.\]

Since \(R'\to R\) is a small extension, \(D^1(R'|R,k)\) is 1-dimensional vector space over \(k\) generated by the element (denoted by \([R']\)) represented by the extension \(0\to k\to R'\to R\to0\). On the other hand, \(A\) is \(R\)-flat and hence

\[D^i(A|R,A_0)=D^i(A_0|k,A_0)\quad\text{for all }i>0\]

and in particular \(D^2(A|R,A_0)=0\) by 3.12(ii) since \(A_0\) is locally a complete-intersection. Consequently we get the exact sequence

\[\to D^1(A_0|k,A_0)\to D^1(A|R',A_0)\to D^1(R|R',k)\otimes_k A_0\to0.\]

This means that there exists an \(R'\)-algebra extension of \(A\) by \(A_0\), denoted by \(A'\), such that \([A']\mapsto[R']\otimes1\) under the map \(D^1(A|R',A_0)\to D^1(R|R',k)\otimes_kA_0\), which means that the deformation \((R,\operatorname{Spec}(A))\) can be lifted to a deformation \((R',\operatorname{Spec}(A'))\).

\(b\) By 3.14 we have the isomorphisms

\[D^1(A_0|k,A_0)\simeq D^1((A_0)_x|k,(A_0)_x) \simeq D^1(\widehat{(A_0)_x}|k,\widehat{(A_0)_x}),\]

i.e.,

\[[M_{X_0}(k[\epsilon])]\simeq [M_{\operatorname{Spec}(\mathcal O_{X_0,x})}(k[\epsilon])] \simeq[M_{\operatorname{Spec}(\widehat{\mathcal O}_{X_0,x})}(k[\epsilon])].\]

It suffices to show, by 2.7\(a\), that

\[[M_{X_0}]\to[M_{\operatorname{Spec}(\mathcal O_{X_0,x})}] \to[M_{\operatorname{Spec}(\widehat{\mathcal O}_{X_0,x})}]\]

are both smooth functors. Let \((R,\operatorname{Spec}(A))\) be a deformation of \(X_0=\operatorname{Spec}(A_0)\), and let \(0\to k\to R'\to R\to0\) be a small extension in \(C_\Lambda\). As above, the ring homomorphisms \(R'\to R\to A\) gives us the exact commutative diagram

\[\xymatrix@C=2.4em{ {{}\to D^1(A_0|k,A_0)}\ar[r]\ar[d]^{\sim}&D^1(A|R',A_0)\ar[r]\ar[d]&{D^1(R|R',k)\otimes_kA_0}\ar[r]\ar[d]&D^2(A_0|k,A_0)\ar[r]\ar[d]^{\sim}&\cdots\\ {{}\to D^1(A_0|k,(A_0)_x)}\ar[r]&D^1(A|R',(A_0)_x)\ar[r]&{D^1(R|R',k)\otimes_k(A_0)_x}\ar[r]&D^2(A_0|k,(A_0)_x)\ar[r]&\cdots\\ {{}\to D^1((A_0)_x|k,(A_0)_x)}\ar[r]\ar[u]&D^1(A_x|R',(A_0)_x)\ar[r]^{p_x}\ar[u]&{D^1(R|R',k)\otimes_k(A_0)_x}\ar[r]\ar[u]&D^2((A_0)_x|k,(A_0)_x)\ar[r]\ar[u]&\cdots} \]

where the indicated isomorphism comes from the fact that \(A_0\) is smooth over \(k\) except at one closed point \(x\) so that

\[D^i(A_0|k,(A_0)_x)=D^i(A_0|k,A_0)_x=D^i(A_0|k,A_0)\]

for all \(i>0\). Now suppose that we are given an arrow

\[(R',\operatorname{Spec}(B))\to(R,\operatorname{Spec}(A_x)) \quad\text{in }[M_{\operatorname{Spec}((A_0)_x)}].\]

It means that we are given a class \([B]\in D^1(A_x|R',(A_0)_x)\) such that \(p_x([B])=[R']\). Then a trivial diagram chasing shows that there exists \([A']\in D^1(A|R',A_0)\) such that \(p([A'])=[R']\) and \(A'_x=B\) i.e., \((R',\operatorname{Spec}(A'))\) is a deformation of \(\operatorname{Spec}(A_0)\) to \(R'\) such that \(\operatorname{Spec}(A')_x=\operatorname{Spec}(B)\). This proves that \(M_{X_0}\to M_{\operatorname{Spec}(\mathcal O_{X_0,x})}\) is smooth. The smoothness of the functor

\[M_{\operatorname{Spec}((A_0)_x)}\to M_{\operatorname{Spec}(\widehat{(A_0)_x})}\]

comes from the similar diagram together with the fact that

\[D^i((A_0)_x|k,\widehat{(A_0)_x}) =D^i(\widehat{(A_0)_x}|k,\widehat{(A_0)_x}) =D^i((A_0)_x|k,(A_0)_x)\]

for all \(i>0\) since \(x\) is an only possible non-smooth point.

blocks.title: Theorem 4.11.
blocks.html:
Theorem 4.11.

Let \(X_0\) be a (separated) \(k\)-scheme of finite type, smooth outside a finite closed set \(X_0^{\mathrm{Sing}}=\{x_1,\ldots,x_r\}\) of \(X_0\). Let \(U_0^1,\ldots,U_0^r\) be affine open neighbourhoods of \(x_1,\ldots,x_r\) such that \(U_0^i\cap X_0^{\mathrm{Sing}}=\{x_i\}\). If \(H^2(X_0,D^0_{X_0})=0\), then the natural functor

\[M_{X_0}\to M_{U_0^1}\times\cdots\times M_{U_0^r}\]

is smooth.

Proof: We can complete \(U_0^1,\ldots,U_0^r\) into an affine open covering of \(X_0\) by choosing \(U_0^{r+1},\ldots,U_0^N\) such that \(U_0^j\cap X_0^{\mathrm{Sing}}=\varnothing\) for all \(j>r\). Now let \(R'\to R\) be a small extension in \(C_\Lambda\), and let \((R',U'{}^1),\ldots,(R',U'{}^r)\) be deformations of \(U_0^1,\ldots,U_0^r\) to \(R'\) such that \(U'{}^i\otimes_{R'}R=X|U_0^i\), \(1\leq i\leq r\), for some deformation \((R,X)\) of \(X_0\) to \(R\). We must show that there exists a deformation \((R',X')\) of \(X_0\) to \(R'\) such that \(X'\otimes_{R'}R=X\) and \(X'|U_0^i=U'{}^i\) for \(1\leq i\leq r\). Since \(U_0^j\) for \(j>r\) is smooth over \(k\), \(X|U_0^j\) can be lifted, by 4.9\(a\), to a deformation \((R',U'{}^j)\), i.e., there exist deformations \((R',U'{}^j)\) of \(U_0^j\) to \(R'\) such that \(U'{}^j\otimes_{R'}R=X|U_0^j\) for all \(j>r\). Since, for any pair \(i\neq j\), \(U_0^i\cap U_0^j\) is affine and smooth over \(k\), we obtain an isomorphism

\[\tau_{i,j}:U'{}^i|U_0^i\cap U_0^j\xrightarrow\sim U'{}^j|U_0^i\cap U_0^j\]

such that \(\tau_{i,j}\otimes_{R'}R=\operatorname{id}_{X|U_0^i\cap U_0^j}\), and in turn defines a cohomology class in \(H^2(X_0,D^0_{X_0})\) given by

\[U_0^i\cap U_0^j\cap U_0^k\longmapsto \tau_{i,j}\tau_{j,k}\tau_{i,k}^{-1}.\]

Since \(H^2(X_0,D^0_{X_0})=0\) by hypothesis, the obstruction for glueing these to obtain a global deformation vanishes, and hence we get a deformation \((R',X')\) of \(X_0\) to \(R'\) such that \(X'\otimes_{R'}R=X\) and \(X'|U_0^j\simeq U'{}^j\). This completes our proof.

Remark 4.12. The previous proof assumes \(X_0\) to be separated, which is unnecessary. In fact, for a fixed deformation \((R,X)\) of \(X_0\) and deformations \((R',U'{}^j)\) of \(U_0^j\), the deformations \((R',V')\) of open set \(V\) of \(X\) to \(R'\) compatible with the deformation \((R',U'{}^j)\) already given, form a “gerbe” (in Giraud's terminology) whose structure sheaf is \(D^0_{X_0}\). Hence the obstruction to the existence of a section of this “gerbe” (i.e., a global deformation \((R',X')\) of \(X\) compatible with the given datum \((R',U'{}^j)\)) is in \(H^2(X_0,D^0_{X_0})\) which is zero by assumption.

blocks.title: Corollary 4.13.
blocks.html:
Corollary 4.13.

Let \(X_0\) be a \(k\)-scheme of finite type, smooth outside a finite subset \(X_0^{\mathrm{Sing}}=\{x_1,\ldots,x_r\}\) of \(X_0\) such that \(H^2(X_0,D^0_{X_0})=0\). Then

\(a\) \(M_{X_0}\to M_{\operatorname{Spec}(\widehat{\mathcal O}_{X_0,x_1})} \times\cdots\times M_{\operatorname{Spec}(\widehat{\mathcal O}_{X_0,x_r})}\) is smooth. In particular,

\[\underline{\dim}\,M_{X_0}=\dim H^1(X_0,D^0_{X_0})+ \sum_{i=1}^r\underline{\dim}\,M_{\operatorname{Spec}(\widehat{\mathcal O}_{X_0,x_i})}.\]

\(b\) If furthermore \(X_0\) is locally a complete-intersection, then \(M_{X_0}\) is smooth.

Proof:

\(a\) The first statement follows from 4.10 and 4.9\(b\). Now let \((R,\mathcal X)\) and \((R_1,\mathcal X_1),\ldots,(R_r,\mathcal X_r)\) be formal versal deformations of \(X_0\) and \(\operatorname{Spec}(\widehat{\mathcal O}_{X_0,x_1}),\ldots, \operatorname{Spec}(\widehat{\mathcal O}_{X_0,x_r})\) respectively. We then obtain a commutative diagram

\[\xymatrix@C=4em@R=3em{ h_R\ar[r]\ar[d]&h_{R_1\widehat\otimes_\Lambda\cdots\widehat\otimes_\Lambda R_r}\ar[d]\\ M_{X_0}\ar[r]&M_{\operatorname{Spec}(\widehat{\mathcal O}_{X_0,x_1})}\times\cdots\times M_{\operatorname{Spec}(\widehat{\mathcal O}_{X_0,x_r})}} \]

Since the composite functor

\[h_R\to M_{X_0}\to M_{\operatorname{Spec}(\widehat{\mathcal O}_{X_0,x_1})} \times\cdots\times M_{\operatorname{Spec}(\widehat{\mathcal O}_{X_0,x_r})}\]

is smooth whereas

\[h_{R_1\widehat\otimes_\Lambda\cdots\widehat\otimes_\Lambda R_r}\to M_{\operatorname{Spec}(\widehat{\mathcal O}_{X_0,x_1})}\times\cdots\times M_{\operatorname{Spec}(\widehat{\mathcal O}_{X_0,x_r})}\]

is minimally smooth, it follows from 2.7\(a\) that

\[h_R\to h_{R_1\widehat\otimes_\Lambda\cdots\widehat\otimes_\Lambda R_r}\]

is smooth i.e., \(R\) is a formal power-series ring over \(R_1\widehat\otimes_\Lambda\cdots\widehat\otimes_\Lambda R_r\). Since

\[0\to H^1(X_0,D^0_{X_0})\to[M_{X_0}(k[\epsilon])]\to [M_{\operatorname{Spec}(\widehat{\mathcal O}_{X_0,x_1})}(k[\epsilon])] \cdots[M_{\operatorname{Spec}(\widehat{\mathcal O}_{X_0,x_r})}(k[\epsilon])]\]

is exact by 4.4 i.e.,

\[0\to H^1(X_0,D^0_{X_0})\to\operatorname{Hom}_{\Lambda\text{-alg}}(R,k[\epsilon]) \to\operatorname{Hom}_{\Lambda\text{-alg}} (R_1\widehat\otimes_\Lambda\cdots\widehat\otimes_\Lambda R_r,k[\epsilon])\]

is exact, it follows that \(R\) is a formal power-series ring over \(R_1\widehat\otimes_\Lambda\cdots\widehat\otimes_\Lambda R_r\) in \(d\)-variables where \(d=\dim_k H^1(X_0,D^0_{X_0})\). Therefore

\[\begin{aligned} \underline{\dim}\,M_{X_0}=\dim k\widehat\otimes_\Lambda R &=d+\dim k\widehat\otimes_\Lambda (R_1\widehat\otimes_\Lambda\cdots\widehat\otimes_\Lambda R_r)\\ &=d+\dim((k\widehat\otimes_\Lambda R_1)\widehat\otimes_k\cdots \widehat\otimes_k(k\widehat\otimes_\Lambda R_r))\\ &=d+\sum_{i=1}^r\dim k\widehat\otimes_\Lambda R_i =d+\sum_{i=1}^r\underline{\dim}\, M_{\operatorname{Spec}(\widehat{\mathcal O}_{X_0,x_i})}. \end{aligned}\]
\(b\) follows from 4.10 and 4.9\(a\).

4.14. We close this paragraph with an explicit construction of a formal versal deformation in a simple situation. In fact, if \(X_0\) is affine and is a complete- intersection, it is trivial to write down explicitly. Thus let
\[A_0=k[x_1,\ldots,x_n]/(f_1,\ldots,f_r)=S/J\]
where \(S=k[x_1,\ldots,x_n]\), \(J=(f_1,\ldots,f_r)\), and \(f_1,\ldots,f_r\) is an \(S\)-regular sequence. We then obtain the exact sequence
\[\cdots\to\operatorname{Der}_k(S,A_0)\to \operatorname{Hom}_{A_0}(J/J^2,A_0)\to D^1(A_0|k,A_0)\to0.\]

We note that \(J/J^2\) is \(A_0\)-free module of rank \(r\) generated by \(f_1\pmod {J^2},\ldots,f_r\pmod {J^2}\), since \(f_1,\ldots,f_r\) is an \(S\)-regular sequence. We now assume that \(D^1(A_0|k,A_0)\) is a finite-dimensional vector space over \(k\), say \(A\) has isolated non-smooth points only. Let \(d=\dim_kD^1(A_0|k,A_0)\) and choose
\[p_j:J/J^2\to A_0\qquad(j=1,2,\ldots,d)\]

which form a \(k\)-basis of \(D^1(A_0|k,A_0)\). Now choose \(P_{ji}\in\Lambda[x_1,\ldots,x_n]\) such that \(p_j(f_i)=P_{ji}\pmod {\mathfrak m_\Lambda+J}\) where \(\mathfrak m_\Lambda\) is the maximal ideal of \(\Lambda\). We then consider
\[R=\Lambda[[t_1,\ldots,t_d]],\qquad B=R[x_1,\ldots,x_n]/(G_1,\ldots,G_r)\]

where \(G_j=F_j+\sum_{i=1}^d t_iP_{ji}\) and \(F_j\) is a polynomial with coefficients in \(\Lambda\) gotten from \(f_j\) by lifting the coefficients from \(k\) to \(\Lambda\). Since \(R[x_1,\ldots,x_n]\) is \(R\)-flat and \(G_1,\ldots,G_r\) modulo \(\mathfrak m_R\) (which becomes \(f_1,\ldots,f_r\)) is a regular sequence in \(k\otimes_\Lambda R[x_1,\ldots,x_n]\), it follows that \(B\) is \(R\)-flat, and \(k\otimes_RB=A_0\). Furthermore,
\[\operatorname{Hom}_{\Lambda\text{-alg}}(R,k[\epsilon])\to D^1(A_0|k,A_0) \simeq[M_{\operatorname{Spec}(A_0)}(k[\epsilon])]\]
is bijective by our construction of \(B\). Therefore, if we set
\[\operatorname{Spf}(B)=\varprojlim_v\operatorname{Spec}(R/\mathfrak m^{v+1}\otimes_RB)\]

then \((R,\operatorname{Spf}(B))\) is an object in \(\widehat M_{\operatorname{Spec}(A_0)}\) and the functor \(h_R\to\widehat M_{\operatorname{Spec}(A_0)}\) induced by \((R,\operatorname{Spf}(B))\) is smooth by 1.6, since \(R\) is a formal power-series ring over \(\Lambda\).
